entry.title: Welcome to my blog
entry.content:
\(\color{cyan}{Welcome To My Blog!}\)

Introduction

I've been trying to document things about myself, as a reminder that I have existed in this world.

In this blog, I might write about personal feelings, reading notes, thoughts, achievements, plans, challenges...etc

Welcome to my blog!

Have A Look Around!

  • To view \(\color{green}{daily thoughts}\), click \(\href{https://wfyishere.github.io/categories/thoughts/}{<Here>}\)

  • To view \(\color{green}{notes}\), click \(\href{https://wfyishere.github.io/categories/Notes/}{<Here>}\)

  • To view some \(\color{green}{interesting challenges}\) that I've done, click \(\href{https://wfyishere.github.io/categories/Challenges/}{<Here>}\)

  • To view some \(\color{green}{Experiences}\), click \(\href{https://wfyishere.github.io/categories/Experiences/}{<Here>}\)

I also have a list of notes for all the questions that I've tried on \(\color{green}{Leetcode}\), click \(\href{https://wfyishere.github.io/categories/Solutions/LeetCode/}{<Here>}\)

Introduction to Functionalities of the Website

It's adviced to view this website on your computer(to fully make use of the functionalities)

Sidebar

Menu

On the left side of the website, the side bar, you can click on the different directories on the menu such as About, Categories, Tags, Archives, To Do, Search

  • About : Gives basic information about the website and me.

  • Categories : Page where you can check the different types of posts, such as Notes/Thoughts/Challenges etc. Subcategories are provided.

  • Tags : Labels different posts such as "MachineLearning" etc.

  • Archives : Overview of all the posts on the website in time order.

  • To do : Plans for what I want to write / functions that I want to implement in the future. Feel free to give advice!

  • Search : Search specific contents that you want to look for.

Table of Contents

When you view an article, you can click on the sessions under Table of Contents to go to different chapters of the post.

Globe

If you're using a laptop, you might notice the 'Globe' at the bottom of the sidebar. This shows the places where all the visitors of the website came from! Try put your cursor on the globe and zoom in/out using your mouse! Each red dot symbolizes one user!

Posts

  1. Click on read more to read full content of each post!

  2. Word count and estimated Reading time are displayed under the title of each post.

  3. If you find my post interesting, feel free to click on the social media icons at the end of each post to share this post!

  4. You can login your github account to comment each post.

Bottome Bar

The bottom bar provides information about the total wordcount / estimated reading time / the days this website has survived.

Thank Next & Hexo for powering this website.

entry.title: [LeetCode] 417 - Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
entry.content:
Medium level question with BFS and DFS solution.

Problem Description

There is an m x n rectangular island that borders both the Pacific Ocean and Atlantic Ocean. The Pacific Ocean touches the island's left and top edges, and the Atlantic Ocean touches the island's right and bottom edges.

The island is partitioned into a grid of square cells. You are given an m x n integer matrix heights where heights[r][c] represents the height above sea level of the cell at coordinate (r, c).

The island receives a lot of rain, and the rain water can flow to neighboring cells directly north, south, east, and west if the neighboring cell's height is less than or equal to the current cell's height. Water can flow from any cell adjacent to an ocean into the ocean.

Return a 2D list of grid coordinates result where result[i] = [ri, ci] denotes that rain water can flow from cell (ri, ci) to both the Pacific and Atlantic oceans.

\(1 <= m, n <= 200\)

\(0 <= heights[r][c] <= 10^5\)

Solution1: BFS

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n * m), Memory: O(n * m)

Runtime: 10ms, Memory: 45.2MB

Idea & Code

Idea: use BFS for both pacific and atlantic.

Solution 2: DFS

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(mn), Memory: O(nm)

Runtime: 4ms, Memory: 45.6MB

Idea & Code

Idea: Use DFS for both pacific and atlantic

Conclusion

Interesting question, help me understand BFS and DFS more.

Time used: 80 minutes.

entry.title: [LeetCode] 647 - Palindromic Substrings
entry.content:
Dynamic Programmning easy question.

Problem Description

Given a string s, return the number of palindromic substrings in it.

A string is a palindrome when it reads the same backward as forward.

A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within the string.

\(1 <= s.length <= 1000\)

Solution: Dynamic Programming

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n ^ 2), Memory: O(n ^ 2)

Runtime: 13ms, Memory: 45.40MB

Idea & Code

Idea: Use dp[i][j] to indicate whether the substring from i to j is a palindrome. Then we can use the substring of the substring to check whether the substring is a palindrome.

Note: Bruteforce solution is run through every substrings and check whether they are palindrome. This would take O(n^3) times. We optimize the bruteforce solution by optimizing the process of checking palindrome.

Conclusion

Interesting question.

Time used: 30 minutes.

entry.title: [LeetCode] 19 - Remove Nth Node From End of List
entry.content:
Intuitive but not intuitive solution.

Problem Description

Given the head of a linked list, remove the nth node from the end of the list and return its head.

\(\text{Number of Nodes in list: 1 - 30}\)

Solution(One Pass)

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n), Memory: O(1)

Runtime: 0ms, Memory: 41.92MB

Idea & Code

Idea: Keep a pointer count, which serves at a counter that tells us when curr has reached the last Nth node.

We need curr to walk len - n + 1 steps, and the way we achieve this in one pass is that we first make count walk n steps, then we make curr walk until count reached the end of node.

Conclusion

Very interesting question.

Time used: 20 minutes.

entry.title: [LeetCode] 200 - Number of Islands
entry.content:
Graph question with DFS and BFS

Problem Description

Given an m x n 2D binary grid grid which represents a map of '1's (land) and '0's (water), return the number of islands.

An island is surrounded by water and is formed by connecting adjacent lands horizontally or vertically. You may assume all four edges of the grid are all surrounded by water.

\(1 <= m, n <= 300\)

Solution 1: DFS

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n * m), Memory: O(n * m)

Runtime: 3ms, Memory: 49.12MB

Idea & Code

Idea: Use DFS to find all islands.

Solution 2 : BFS

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n * m), Memory: O(n * m * n * m)

Runtime: 4ms, Memory: 51.9MB

Idea & Code

Idea: Use BFS to do the search

  1. Do note that here we have to BFS each island instead of BFS the whole map. If we want to do searching on the whole map, we can only use DFS here. This is because for a structure like

    1 1 1

    0 1 0

    1 1 1

    BFS the whole map would give us 2 as answer.

  2. Do note that on line A, we need to change grid[newX][newY] immediately after we push new node into the queue, instead of changing the value on line B. This is because queue is a first-in-last-out structure, and if we do not change the value immediately, we would push more same Pair(newX, newY) nodes into the queue before changing the value of grid[newX][newY]. This would ultimately lead to a huge amount of increase in runtime.

Conclusion

Very interesting question. Need rethink before we start to code.

There are a lot of things to consider, like the 2 notes in BFS and the choice of DFS.

Time used: 90 minutes.

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: 5ms, Memory: 44.36MB

Conclusion

entry.title: [LeetCode] 11 - Container With Most Water
entry.content:
Easy question but I didn't came up with an O(n) answer.

Problem Description

You are given an integer array height of length n. There are n vertical lines drawn such that the two endpoints of the ith line are (i, 0) and (i, height[i]).

Find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container, such that the container contains the most water.

Return the maximum amount of water a container can store.

Notice that you may not slant the container.

\(2 <= n <= 10^5\)

\(0 <= height[i] <= 10^4\)

Solution: Bruteforce

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n^2), Memory: O(1)

Runtime: Time Limit Exceeded

Idea & Code

Idea: Bruteforce the solution

Solution: Two Pointers

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n), Memory: O(n)

Runtime: 2ms, Memory: 56.92MB

Idea & Code

Idea: Container = distance * height. Thus, if we want a non-O(n) solution, we would be required to sort either distance or height of the containers. If we sort height, it would be difficult because height is determined by the smallest of them. Thus we need to sort the containers with distance. That's why we keep two pointers from two ends and gradually shrink it to where the two pointers each.

Conclusion

Need to think more.

Time used: 20 minutes.

entry.title: [LeetCode] 424 - Longest Repeating Character Replacement
entry.content:
interesting question with sliding window technique.

Problem Description

You are given a string s and an integer k. You can choose any character of the string and change it to any other uppercase English character. You can perform this operation at most k times.

Return the length of the longest substring containing the same letter you can get after performing the above operations.

\(1 <= s.length <= 105\)

Solution

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n), Memory: O(1)

Runtime: 9ms, Memory: 42.00B

Idea & Code

Idea: Sliding Window Technique.

Sliding Window technique is a frequently used technique in problems involving finding the required continious subarray/substring that fulfills some requirement.

Basically, we use a "window" to iterate throught the whole array/string, we extend the end of the window if requirement is fulfilled and shrink the starting point of the window if requirement is not fulfilled.

The key factor that a sliding window would work(the key factor to prove that the algorithm works) is when if a substring fulfills the requirement, then, any substring of the substring fulfill the requirement.

Here, the requirement is the the minimum amount of characters that we need to change in the substring is smaller than k.

We use start and end to represent the windows. Each time we check whether the current substring fulfills the requirement, until we iterate through the whole array.

Conclusion

i have no idea how to solve this question with O(n) complexity until I learned about the sliding window technique. It's really genius.

Time used: 60minutes.

entry.title: [LeetCode] 153 - Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
entry.content:
A low level version of this

Problem Description

Suppose an array of length n sorted in ascending order is rotated between 1 and n times. For example, the array nums = [0,1,2,4,5,6,7] might become:

[4,5,6,7,0,1,2] if it was rotated 4 times. [0,1,2,4,5,6,7] if it was rotated 7 times. Notice that rotating an array [a[0], a[1], a[2], ..., a[n-1]] 1 time results in the array [a[n-1], a[0], a[1], a[2], ..., a[n-2]].

Given the sorted rotated array nums of unique elements, return the minimum element of this array.

You must write an algorithm that runs in O(log n) time.

\(1 <= n <= 5000\)

\(-5000 <= nums[i] <= 5000\)

Solution

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(log n), Memory: O(n)

Runtime: 2ms, Memory: 56.92MB

Idea & Code

Idea: See this

Conclusion

Lol, I just use the code from here

Time used: 3 minutes.

entry.title: [LeetCode] 141 - Linked List Cycle
entry.content:
Easy question with different solutions

Here is a very interesting and thorough analysis of the question

Problem Description

Given head, the head of a linked list, determine if the linked list has a cycle in it

\(-10^5 <= Node.val <= 10^5\)

\(NumberOfNode:[0, 10^4]\)

Solution 1: HashMap

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n), Memory: O(n)

Runtime: 5ms, Memory: 44.5MB

Idea & Code

Idea: Iterate through the list. Use HashMap to store whether the current node has been visited

Solution 2: Visited Node

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n), Memory: O(1)

Runtime: 1ms, Memory: 44.1MB

Idea & Code

Idea: Iterate through the list. Use a Node end to denote that the node has been visited.

Solution 3: Runner Technique

Idea: If we keep two pointers slow and fast, where slow moves 1 step at a time and fast moves 2 steps at a time. If a loop exists, at some point slow will meet fast. (Imagine the loop as a clock!)

Runtime: O(n) Space Complexity: O(1)

Code

Conclusion

All three solutions are interesting. For the first two solutions, we are somehow trapped in the mindset of "trying to label each visited node", while the third approcah jumps out of that mindset.

I am amzed by the third approach, and when I try to think of how the inventor of the approach came up with the solution, here is perhaps what he'd thought: (Don't blaim me for over-analyzing)

  1. When we iterate through the linked-list, we realize that we find ourselves trapped in the loop when we realize that we've been iterating for a long time

  2. However, in real life we cannot realize that we've been iterating for a long time. Thus we need something to tell us that "If there was no loop, you would have stopped iterating!" -> then somehow Floyd came up with the two pointers

Time used: 30 minutes.

entry.title: [LeetCode] 206 - Reverse a Linked List
entry.content:
I wasn't too familiar with linked list. still treated it as a list.

Idea: Simple recursion

Solution 2: Iteration

Runtime: 0ms, Memory: 42.08MB

Idea: Simple Iteration

Conclusion

Well I guess that iteration and recursion should always be considered simultaneously.

entry.title: [LeetCode] 33 - Search in Rotated Sorted Array
entry.content:
Simple Question with binarySearch.

Problem Description

There is an integer array nums sorted in ascending order (with distinct values).

Prior to being passed to your function, nums is possibly rotated at an unknown pivot index k (1 <= k < nums.length) such that the resulting array is [nums[k], nums[k+1], ..., nums[n-1], nums[0], nums[1], ..., nums[k-1]] (0-indexed). For example, [0,1,2,4,5,6,7] might be rotated at pivot index 3 and become [4,5,6,7,0,1,2].

Given the array nums after the possible rotation and an integer target, return the index of target if it is in nums, or -1 if it is not in nums.

You must write an algorithm with O(log n) runtime complexity

\(1 <= nums.length <= 5000\)

\(-10^4 <= nums[i] <= 10^4\)

Solution

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(log n), Memory: O(1)

Runtime: 0ms, Memory: 42.08MB

Idea & Code

Idea: First use binary search to find out where the array was rotated, then binary search on the unrotated subarray.

Conclusion

Simple question with binary search.

Time used: 20 minutes.

entry.title: [LeetCode] 49 - Group Anagrams
entry.content:
Interesting question... How could I have not thought of hashMap

Problem Description

Given an array of strings strs, group the anagrams together. You can return the answer in any order.

An Anagram is a word or phrase formed by rearranging the letters of a different word or phrase, typically using all the original letters exactly once.

\(1 <= strs.length <= 10^4\)

\(0 <= strs[i].length <= 100\)

Solution 1 HashMap

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n), Memory: O(n), where n is the number of strings

Runtime: 7ms, Memory: 47.51MB

Idea & Code

Idea: We use HashMap to store the anagrams and use the string itself as key of the hashMap. This greatly imporves runtime.

Solution 2 BruteForce

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n^2), Memory: O(n), where n is the number of strings

Runtime: 890ms, Memory: 47.02MB

Idea & Code

Idea: Bruteforce the question.

Conclusion

How could I have not thought of HASHMAP!

I think that we should think about using hashMap whenever we are using a data structure that requires QUICKFINDING! E.g. here we need quickfind of Anagrams.

Or maybe we should think of hashMap whenever we need to iterate through a whole array.

Also, here we can see that the length of the string is at most 100. Thus, we can use sorting algorithm on the strings.

Time used: minutes.

entry.title: [LeetCode] 152 - Maximum Product Subarray
entry.content:
Easy problem but took me soooo long!

Problem Description

Given an integer array nums, find a subarray that has the largest product, and return the product.

The test cases are generated so that the answer will fit in a 32-bit integer.

\(1 <= nums.length <= 2 * 10^4\)

\(-10 <= nums[i] <= 10\)

Solution 1 : Mathematical Solution(Best)

See Original Blog here

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n), Memory: O(1)

Runtime: 1ms, Memory: 44.70MB

Idea & Code

Idea: For any array,. the absolute value of the product is always increasing. Thus, the maximum array would have the following two features the maximum of all arrays that

  1. product is positive (negative for special cases)

  2. one end of the array is the "edge" of the array (we define edge as either the starting/ending point of the array or i where nums[i] = 0)

Solution 2 : Bruteforce

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n), Memory: O(1)

Runtime: 1ms, Memory: 44.67MB

Idea & Code

Idea: TLDR, Bruteforce the solution with negativeMax and positiveMax.

This is a very bad code, please don't read it. It NEEDS improvement but I'm just too tired to do that.

Conclusion

This is actually a very easy question but I spent 80 minutes debugging! I feel like my coding skills still needs improving.

BTW, still beaten by easy mathematical solution.

Time used: 80minutes.

entry.title: [LeetCode] 56 - Merge Intervals
entry.content:
Easy problem, helps me understand more about sorting and arrays in java.

Problem Description

Given an array of intervals where intervals[i] = [starti, endi], merge all overlapping intervals, and return an array of the non-overlapping intervals that cover all the intervals in the input.

\(1 <= intervals.length <= 10^4\)

\(0 <= starti <= endi <= 10^4\)

Solution

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n log n), Memory: O(n)

Runtime: 7ms, Memory: 46.53MB

Idea & Code

Idea: The main problem that we need to solve here is that we need to sort the intervals in ascending order of the starting points. Here we use a simple comparator.

Once the intervals are sorted, we just need to intuitively merge the intervals one by one.

Conclusion

At first I was thinking of divide and conquer and maybe we can use an idea similar to that of merge sort. We first "merge" the two parts of the array(that is, merge all overlapping intervals), then try to merge the two "merged" parts. However, when we try the 2nd step(merging the two parts), we would need the two parts to be both sorted. At that time I finally realize that I have made the question too complicated and that we just need to sort the intervals and intuitively merge them.

Time used: 40 minutes.

entry.title: [Leetcode] 15 - 3Sum
entry.content:
An extended version of Two-Sum

Problem Description

Given an integer array nums, return all the triplets [nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]] such that i != j, i != k, and j != k, and nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k] == 0.

Notice that the solution set must not contain duplicate triplets.

\(3 <= nums.length <= 3000\)

\(-10^5 <= nums[i] <= 10^5\)

Solution

Solution 1: hashMap Solution

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n^2), Memory: O(n^2)

Runtime: 508ms, Memory: 51.6MB

Idea & Code

Idea:

  1. Sort the array

  2. Iterate throught the array and find the Two-Sum of -nums[i].

Solution 2: Low-High Pointer

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n^2), Memory: O(n)

Runtime: 31ms, Memory: 51.43MB

Idea & Code

The Low-High Pointer approach would be a better solution. That is because, though both Low-High Pointer and HashMap takes O(n) time, we need extra space for HashMap solution to make sure that the array includes no duplicates.

Conclusion

Interesting question. Made me realize that I should consider the complexity(in terms of time and difficulty) before choosing an approach. E.g. Low-High Pointer is significantly easier approach here.

Time used: 40 minutes.

entry.title: [LeetCode] 238 - Product of Array Except Self
entry.content:
Easy question but I didn't think much about it before looking for solutions :C

Problem Description

Given an integer array nums, return an array answer such that answer[i] is equal to the product of all the elements of nums except nums[i].

The product of any prefix or suffix of nums is guaranteed to fit in a 32-bit integer.

You must write an algorithm that runs in O(n) time and without using the division operation.

\(2 <= nums.length <= 10^5\)

\(-30 <= nums[i] <= 30\) \(\text{The product of any prefix or suffix of nums is guaranteed to fit in a 32-bit integer.}\)

Solution

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n), Memory: O(1)

Runtime: 1ms, Memory: 55.40MB

Idea & Code

Idea: use two arrays pre and suff to store product of all the prefix of nums[i] and all the suffix of nums[i]. Multiply them.

Optimization: instead of using pre or suff as arrays, we store them directly integers.

Conclusion

This is a simple question but I've missed the interesting part of it because I peeked at the solution first QwQ.

Time used: 10 minutes.

entry.title: [LeetCode] 53 - Maximum Subarray
entry.content:
Simple question with different ways of thinking

Problem Description

Given an integer array nums, find the subarray with the largest sum, and return its sum. Subarrays should contain at least one element of the array and should be consistent.

\(1 <= nums.length <= 10^5\)

\(-10^4 <= nums[i] <= 10^4\)

Solution

Bruteforce Solution

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n), Memory: O(1)

Runtime: 1ms, Memory: 56.91MB

Idea & Code

Idea: the sum of a subarray i to j is sum[j] - sum[i - 1]. If we use min to store the minimum of the j sums, The maximum subarray that contains the jth element should be sum[j] - min. Thus. we use a single for loop to iterate through the array, and each time we update min and sum[i]. Finally, we use max to store the maximum subarray of 0 -> ith elements.

**Optimization: We can use currentSum to store the current sums instead of using the sums array.*

Best Solution

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n), Memory: O(1)

Runtime: 1ms, Memory: 57.16MB

Idea & Code

Idea lets say for any subarray with the largest sum largest. For any k in largest, sum of [0...k] should not be negative, or else the sum of largest[k + 1...] would be larger.

Thus we just have to calculate and check any continued subarray whose sum > 0. If at some point the current sum is smaller than zero, we just need to restart.

Suggested Solutions

Inspired by this blog

Recursion

Idea: At each index i, we either pick the element or not pick it. We use a helper function solve(int index, boolean pick) to denote the maximum subarray of the nums[index : nums.length](that is, the subarray from index onwards) when we pick the nums[index](if pick == true, we must pick the element, if pick == false we can either pick the element or not pick it.).

Thus, if pick == true, solve = Math.max(0, nums[index] + solve(array, index + 1, true)). This is because if we pick one element, we must pick the following elements as well.

If pick == false, solve = max(solve(index + 1, false), A[i] + solve(index + 1, true)). That is, the maximum of either we pick the next element or not.

Runtime Analysis: runtime: O(n ^2), Space: O(n)

Proof: suppose T1(n) denotes the runtime of running solve(0, true), while T2(n) denotes the runtime of running solve(0, false).

  • \(T1(n) = 1 + T1(n - 1) -> T1(n) = O(n)\)

  • \(T2(n) = T1(n-1) + T2(n - 1) = O(n) + T2(n - 1) = O(n ^ 2)\)

Optimization: We use memoization to improve the behavior of solve. This leads to the Dynamic Programming Solution.

Dynamic Programming

Idea: We use dp[0][n] to denote solve(0, false), use dp[1][n] to denote solve(0, true).

Runtime Analysis: Runtime: O(n), Memory: O(n)

Here we calculate every index from 0 to n - 1 of solve(index, true)and solve(index, false) once. Thus we optimize the runtime to O(n).

Divide & Conquer

Idea: We realize that the subarrays either lie entirely on the left or right half of the array, or lies in the middle. Thus, if we use a helper function solve(int left, int right) to denote the maximum sum of the subarray of nums[left: right], it would be the maximum of the three cases.

For the thrid case where the subarray lies between the left and right half, we can calculate the sum of

  • maximum subarray sum in [left, mid - 1] and starting from mid - 1

  • nums[mid]

  • maximum subarray sum in mid + 1, right and starting from mid + 1.

Runtime Analysis Runtime: O(n log n), Memory: O(n)

Proof: Use T(n) to denote calculating solve for an array of length n.

\(T(n) = 2 * T(n / 2) + O(n) = O(n log n)\). Where the first two cases are T(n / 2) and the third case takes \(O(n)\) (iterating through the whole array) .

Conclusion

A very interesting question. I come up with my own solution and then find out there were so many interesting solutions out there(dp/divide/recursion etc.)! However, all these were defeated by the best solution! Sometimes mathematics is much more useful than CS!

Time used: 40 minutes.

entry.title: [LeetCode] 20 - Valid Parentheses
entry.content:
Easy question but a lot of special cases to think about.

Problem Description

Given a string s containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid.

An input string is valid if:

Open brackets must be closed by the same type of brackets.

Open brackets must be closed in the correct order.

Every close bracket has a corresponding open bracket of the same type.

\(1 <= s.length <= 104\)

Solution

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n), Memory: O(n)

Runtime: 1ms, Memory: 43.21MB

Idea & Code

Idea: Maintain a stack. Everytime a left parenthesis is met, push into stack. If right parenthesis is met, check if the top component in stack is the correct left parenthesis. if yes, pop the top component. Else, return false. Finally, check whether the stack is empty.

Here we use an array and a pointer to implement stack.

Conclusion

This is a quite easy question, but there are a lot of special cases. E.g. "[" should returen false(reason why the final return statement is pointer == -1).

At first I tried not using stacks, and instead just use an array parnthesis[3] to check whether the number of left parentheses are correct. However, that did not work as cases like "[(])" is considered false. That's why we have to use stack structure.

Time used: 20 minutes.

entry.title: [LeetCode] 242 - Valid Anagram
entry.content:
Easy question. Help me understand strings in java more.

Problem Description

Given two strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s, and false otherwise.

An Anagram is a word or phrase formed by rearranging the letters of a different word or phrase, typically using all the original letters exactly once.

\(1 <= s.length, t.length <= 5 * 10^4\)

\(\text{s and t consist of lowercase English letters.}\)

Solution

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n), Memory: O(n)

Runtime: 5ms, Memory: 42.61MB

Idea & Code

Idea: Brute force. Use an array asciis[i] to store the number of appearance of the char with ascii code i. add ascii[i] if the char appeared in s, minus if appeared in t. At last, check if all ascii[i] = 0.

Possible Solutions

Sorting

Idea: Sort s and t, check if the two strings are equal

Hash Table

Idea: use hashtable instead of ascii in the last solution

Conclusion

Time used: 10 minutes.

entry.title: [LeetCode] 121 - Best Time to Buy and Seel Stock
entry.content:
Brute Force Easy Question

Problem Description

You are given an array prices where prices[i] is the price of a given stock on the ith day.

You want to maximize your profit by choosing a single day to buy one stock and choosing a different day in the future to sell that stock.

Return the maximum profit you can achieve from this transaction. If you cannot achieve any profit, return 0.

\(1 <= prices.length <= 10^9\)

\(0 <= prices[i] <= 10^4\)

Solution

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n), Memory: O(n)

Runtime: 2ms, Memory: 56.92MB

Idea & Code

Idea: Brute Force. Use maxNumber[i] to store the maximum price since day i, and try to find out largest maxNumber[i+1] - prices[i]

Conclusion

Easy question. Time used: 10 minutes.

entry.title: [LeetCode] 1 - Two Sum
entry.content:
Easy problem but various solutions.

Problem Description

Find the problem description here

Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target.

Each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice.

\(2 <= nums.length <= 10^4\)

\(-10^9 <= nums[i] <= 10^9\)

\(-10^9 <= target <= 10^9\)

Solutions (with code)

BruteForce

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n^2), Memory: O(n)

Runtime: 45ms, Memory: 44.89MB

Idea & Code

use two for loops to search through all two numbers whose sum might match the target

Sorting + Low-High Pointer

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(nlogn), Memory: O(n)

Runtime: 9ms, Moemry: 44.60MB

Analysis: Sorting: O(n log n), two pointers: O(n)

Idea & Code

  1. Sort the array. To compensate for the information lost during the search, that is, the index of the array, we use pair to store the array

  2. Keep two pointers low and high. Initially, the two pointers points to the smallest and largest numbers in the array. For each time, we check if the sum of the two numbers that the pointers are pointing two is the target that we need. If the sum is smaller, we move the low pointer rightwards, if the sum is larger, we move the high pointer leftwards.

Suggested Solutions

Sorting + QuickFind

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n log n), Space complexity: O(n)

Analysis: Sorting: O(n log n), QuickFind: O(log n) for each time, run n times, thus O(n log n).

Idea

  1. Sort the array.

  2. For each number in the array, we use QuickFind to find the target - number

HashMap

Please check here for detailed code.

Runtime Analysis

Runtime: O(n), Space Complexity: O(1) (\(10^9\))

Runtime: 5ms, Space Complexity: 44.55MB

Idea

Use hashmap to store each components in the array, and try to find target - number in hashmap

Optimization: We don't preprocess hashmap then search for the complement of the current number. Instead, we iterate through the array only once and push current number into the hashmap after checking whether the complement of current is in the hashmap.

Note: Do note that here, we store nums[i] as key and i as value, since we search using nums[i]

Conclusion

This is an interesting problem, get to know more about arrays and hashmaps.

Time Taken: 42 minutes (including two solutions).
