entry.title: GEA1000N Lecture Notes
entry.content:
This for a SU mode? Lol

Chapter 1: Getting Data

  1. \(Population\) \(Parameter\): A numerical fact about the population(e.g. standard deviation of the average height)

  2. \(Sampling\) \(Frame\): list from which the \(sample\)(opposed to \(census\)) is selected

  3. \(Bias\)

    • \(Selection\) \(Bias\): the researcher's biased selection of sample or non-probability sampling

    • \(Non-response\) \(Bias\): participant's non-disclosure/non-participation

  4. \(Probability\) \(Sampling\): each unit in the sample has a known non-zero probability of being selected.

    • \(Simple\) \(Random\) \(Sampling\): each set of n-units has the same chance of being selected. Here we use \(sampling\) \(without\) \(replacement\).

    • \(Systematic\) \(Sampling\): applying a selection interval k and a random starting point from the first interval

    • \(Stratified\) \(Sampling\): sampling frame is divided into groups called strata. Each stratum is similar in that they share similar characteristics but the size of each stratum does not necessarily have to be the same.

    • \(Cluster\) \(Sampling\):A fixed number of clusters are then selected using simple random sampling. All the units from the selected clusters are then included in the overall sample.

    • \(Non-probability\) \(Sampling\): Convenience sampling / volunteer sampling

  5. \(Variables\)

    • \(Categoriacal\):

      • \(Ordinal\): Natural ordering and numbers can be used to represent the ordering. E.g. Rating

      • \(Nominal\)

    • \(Numerical\)

      • \(Discrete\)

      • \(Continuous\)

  6. \(Summary Statistics\)

    • \(Variance\) and \(Standard-Deviation\) ([U] Why is it not n)

      \[Var = \frac{(x_1 − \bar{x})^2 + (x_2 − \bar{x})^2 + · · · + (x_n − \bar{x})^2}{n−1}\]

      \[S_x = \sqrt{Var}\]

      \[\text{coefficient of variation} = \frac{s_x}{\bar{x}}\]

      Adding a constant C to each data does not change variance, however, multiplying C to the data would make \(S_x'\)

      \[S_x' = |C| S_x\]

    • \(Median\) and \(IQRs\)

      • If you divide the sample into different groups, the overall median would always be between the lowest and highest median of all groups. However, we cannot derive median in this case.

      • Calculating \(Q_1\) and \(Q_3\): divide the group into two halfs using the mdian, than calculate median for the lower half and upper half

      • \(\text{Inter Quatile Range}\): \[IQR = Q_3 - Q_1\]

    • \(Mode\): the data that appeared most.

  7. Studies

    • \(\text{Experimental Study}\):

      • \(placebo\): inactive substance or other intervention given to the control group that looks the same as active drug or treatment being tested. \(Placebo\) \(effect\): refers to when the control group still show some positive effect because of the psychology of believing.

      • \(Blinding\):

    • \(\text{Observational Study}\)

    • Observational Studies cannot provide evidence for cause-and-effect. However, if the experiment has randomised assignment and blinding, it can.

Chapter 2: Categorical Data Analysis

  1. \(rates\)

    • \(marginal\) \(rates\): the rates that are relevant to only 1 categorical value(the rates that uses only the margined values in the \(\text{2x2 contingency table}\))

    • \(conditional\) \(rates\): rate of X given Y

    • \(joint\) \(rates\): rate of (X and Y)

  2. Association

    \[rate(A | B) > rate(A | NB)\]Then, \[\text{there is an positive association between A and B}\]

    \[rate(A | B) < rate(A | NB) ⇔ rate(B | A) < rate(B | NA\tag{Symmetric Rule}\]

    \[\text{rate(A) is between rate(A | B) and rate(A | NB)}\tag{Basic Rule}\], Plus, \[\text{the larger rate(B) is, the closer rate(A) is to rate(A|B)}\].

  3. \(Simpson’s\) \(Paradox\) is a phenomenon in which a trend appears in more than half of the groups of data but disappears or reverses when the groups are combined. This appears because there is a \(confounder\) and can be easily explained using \(Basic\) \(Rule\) and can be solved by \(slicing\) the data or using randomised assignment(remove any of the association of the variables with the confounder).

Dealing with Numerical Data

  1. Describing the shape of a graph

    • \(Peak\): \(Unimodel\) means that the graph has one peak

    • \(Skewness\):

      • left skewed if the tail is on the left. \(Symmetrical\) if it is not skewed.

      • mean < median < mode: left skewed

    • \(Variability\): This can be inferred from the \(Standard Deviation\) and \(range\) of the distribution.

    • \(outliers\): statistical values that are unaffected by errors are called \(robust\) \(statistics\). A data point is an outlier if it is larger than \(Q3 + 1.5 IQR\) or less than \(Q1 - 1.5 IQR\).

      • \(Whiskers\): Extended line from Q1 to the smallest value that is not an outlier and another line from Q3 to the largest value that is not an outlier.

Histogram and Bar Chart are differet, histogram is for numerical variables while bar chart is for categorical variables.

  1. Bivariate EDA

    • Variables

      • \(Deterministic\): The value of one variable can be determined if we know the value of the other variable.

      • \(statistical\): Non-deterministic.

    • \(Direction\): Increase / Decrease / Neither

    • \(Strength\): How closely the data follows the relationship. E.g.: A positive, linear relationship

      • Corelation Coefficient: r. \(-1 <= r <= 1\)

      • Steps for computing Corelation Coefficient:

        1. Convert x and y into standard units by: \[\frac{x - \bar{x}}{s_x}\]

        2. Calculate the Corelation Coefficient by: \[r = \frac{\sum{xy}}{n - 1}\]

      • The correlation coefficient r is not affected by interchanging the x and y variables; adding or multiplying a number to all variables

      • \(ecological\) \(correlation\) represents relationships observed at the aggregate level, considering the characteristics of groups rather than individuals

        • In general, when the association for both individuals and aggregates are in the same direction, the ecological correlation based on aggregates will typically overstate the strength of the association in individuals

        • \(Ecological\) \(Fallacy\): draw wrong conclusion about correlation at the individual level based on what we observe at the aggregate level.

        • \(Atomistic\) \(Fallacy\): Based on the correlation we observed at the individual level, if we had mistakenly concluded that the same correlation would exist at the aggregate level.

  2. Linear Regression

    • \[Y = mX + b\], where b is the y-intercept and m is the slope or gradient of the line.

    \[m =\frac{s_Y}{s_X}r\]

    • \(Residual\): \[\text{residual = actual value - predicted value}\].

Chapter 4: Statistical Inference

  1. \(Statistical\) \(Inference\): drawing conclusion for the population from the sample

    \[\text{Sample statistic = population parameter + bias + random error}\]

  2. \(Probability\) \(Experiment\): An experiment that is repeatable. E.g. Toss a coin twice. A \(Sample\) \(Space\) is the collection of all the \(outcomes\) of he experiment. An \(event\) is the subset of sample space

  3. Three Fallacies

    • \(Prosecutor's\) \(Fallacy\): confusing P(A | B) as P(B | A)

    • \(Conjunction\) \(Fallacy\): P(A ∩ B) > P(A)

    • False Positive

      • \(sensitivity\), or true positive rate \[\text{P(Test positive | Individual is infected)}\]

      • \(specificity\), or true negative rate \[\text{P(Test negative | Individual is not infected)}\]

  4. \(Conditionally\) \(Independent\): \[P(A ∩ B | C) = P(A | C) × P(B | C)\]

  5. \(Random\) \(Variable\) is a numerical variable with probabilities assigned to each of the possible numerical values taken by the numerical variable. ([U] Give an example of non-random variable)

  6. Confidence Interval

    • for the population proportion, \[p^∗ ± z^∗ \sqrt{\frac{p^∗(1 − p^∗)}{n}}\]

      • p∗ = sample proportion

      • z∗ = “z-value” from standard normal distribution

      • n = sample size

    • for the mean, \[\bar{x} ± t \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\]

      • x = sample mean

      • t∗ = “t-value” from t-distribution

      • s = sample standard deviation

      • n = sample size

    • p-value < significance level p-value ≥ significance level - Sufficient evidence to reject null hypothesis in favour of the alternative hypothesis.

    • Insufficient evidence to reject the null hypothesis. The hypothesis test is inconclusive. This does not mean that we accept the null hypothesis.

Terminologies

  • PPDAC: Problem - Plan - Data - Analysis - Conclusion

  • EDA: Exploratory Data Analysis

entry.title: Reading Notes for Pattern Recognizing and Machine Learning by Chris Bishop
entry.content:
This book Pattern Recognizing and Machine Learning by Christopher M. Bishop(2006) was recommended by my professor. Here are the reading notes.

Introduction

Basic Concepts

  1. The ability for the machine leanring algorithm to categorize new examples outside the training set are called generalization, which is the main goal in machine learning.

    • An exmaple of the preprocessing stage is when a machine learning algorithm is used to recognize handwritten digits.

    The images of the digits are typically translated and scaled so that each digit is contained within a box of a fixed size... This pre-processing stage is sometimes also called feature extraction.

  2. reinforcement learning is trying to maximize a reward in a given situation. However, reward has to be attributed appropriately to all the moves that led to it, which is a credit assignment problem. THe general feature of reinforcement learning is thr trade-off between exploration(machine tries out new kinds of actions to see how effective they are) and exploitation (make use of actions that are known to yield a high reward). [U]?

  3. Root-Mean-Square(RMS) error: used to evaluate E(w) for the test data set. where \[E(w) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{n=1}^{N}{(y(x_n, w) - t_n)^2}\]

and RMS is calculated:\[E_{RMS} = \sqrt{2E(w) / N}\]. The square root ensures that \(E_{RMS}\) is measured on the same scale and units as the target vaiable t ([U] Why do we ensure that, also where does the 2 come from)

Since \(E(w)\) is a quadratic function, its derivative would be a linear equation. There should be a one and only w* set where E(w) reaches minimum. We would use w* as our model.

  1. Problem of Overfitting. Suppose we train a model in this way: We use the function \(y = sin(2\pi{x})\), and we generate a training set by adding random noise to the target value. We now train the model with different polynomial functions of \(y = w_0 + w_1 * x^1 + ... + w_M * x^M\), where M denotes the order of the polynomial. We use 3 differnt models with different Ms: M = 0, M = 3, and M = 9. With a training set of 10 data points, we surprisingly find that: M = 0 fits the function \(y = sin(2\pi{x})\) badly, M = 3 fits the function most, M = 9 goes through every point in the data set but it fits the original function badly. We call M = 9 an overfitting.

    • This is quite resonable since for M = 9, there are \(w_0, w_1...w_{10}\) 10 degrees of freedom, so it would fit the training set perfectly. However, if we use RMS error to calculate the performance of different Ms models, we find that M = 9 behaves badly on new datas.

    • This feels strange because (1) M = 3 could be considered a special case for M = 9, so M = 9 should behave at least as good as M = 3. (2) consider the function \(y = sin(2\pi{x})\), it could be represented as a power series, so it was supposed to improve monotonically as we increase M.

    • If we check the w* for each model, we can see that as M increases, w* increases. So we observe oscillation in the polynomial of M = 9. Intuitively, polynomials with larger values of M are increasingly tuned to the random noises.

    • We can see that over-fitting problem becomes less severe as the size of the data set increases. It is said that the number of data points should be no less than some multiple(5/10) to the number of adaptive parmeters in the model. ([u] chapter 3 this changes? [U] over=fitting problem can be understood as "general property of maximum likelihood" ?)

    • This kind of doesn't make sense because we want the number of the parameters in a model(complexity of a model) to be influenced by the complexity of the problem, rather than the number of data points that we have.[U] this can be avoided by the Bayesian model.

    • Without the above approach, one technique to solve the problem is to add an addtional penalty term. \[E(w) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{n=1}^{N}{(y(x_n, w) - t_n)^2 + \lambda{} ||w||^2}\], where \[||w||^2 = w_1^2 + w_2^2+....w_M^2\]. the \(\lambda\) term determines the importance of the two terms. Do note that \(w_0\) is omitted because it causes the results to depend on the choice of origin for the target variable. ([U] or it may be included. Discussed in section 5.5.1). This is called a shrinkage method. The case of quadrati regularizer is called ridge regression, in the context of neural networks, it is called weight decay.

    • Determining the complexity for the model is important. The result above shows a way of seperating two different sets, which is the training data set used to determine w, and a validation set or a hold-out set used to optimize the model complexity.

Probability Theory

TBC.

Updated until page 12. Current plan: one 1.1 everyday.

entry.title: CS2040S Midterm CheatSheet
entry.content:
CheatSheet

  1. Applications of \(binary search\): Think of binary search whenever:
    • There is a sequence of "monotonic function" in blackbox. That is , for each v[i], for i <= k, it returns false, for i >= k, it returns true.
    • We try to find the k which is the seperating point of a "valid" value.
  2. O(log(n!)) == O(nlog(n))

Sorting

Features Bubble Sort Selection Sort Inerstion Sort Merge Sort Quicksort
Time Complexity O(n^2) O(n^2) O(n^2) O(n * log(n)) O(n * log(n))
NearlySorted ? Random O(n) < = O(n) < = [U] undon
Space Complexity O(n) O(n) O(n) O(n) (No need to allocate new arrays, can you previous ones instead) / O(n * log(n)) when it's MergeSort iteration
Stability 1(swap only when elements are different) 0(e.g. [2, 2, 1]) 1(A[i] > key not >=) 1(if implementation is designed carefully) Not stable

  1. \(InsertionSort\): insertion work is done backwards

  2. \(Quicksort\): Partition is done by continuously swapping A[high] < pivot and A[low] > pivot until low >= high. \(Paranoid\) \(Quicksort\) is partition until a good partition.

    • Halt recursion early, leaving small arrays unsorted. Then perform InsertionSort on entire array. (InsertionSort is fast on almost sorted arrays, about O(n))

    • k pivot quicksort. Time complexity is \(O(n log n)\). The performance of 2 and 3 pivot QuickSort are actually better than regular QuickSort because 2-pivot quicksort takes benefits of modern computer architecture.

      • Sort the k pivots in the pivot array K

      • Do QuickSelect(e, K) on every element e in the original array (partitioning)

      • Recurse.

  3. Find \(k^{th}\) smallest element in an unsorted array: Time complexity O(n) [U] Why

Lecture 08 Trees

Dynamic Data Structures

Tree

  1. Successor

    If you search for a key not in a tree, you would either find its successor or its predecessor

  2. Deleting

  3. balanced if h = O(logN)

  4. Find the rank of a node

AVL Trees

  1. node v is height-balanced if |v.left.height – v.right.height| <= 1 -> every height-balanced tree with n nodes has height h < 2log(n).

Interesting Property: a maximal imbalanced AVL tree, with height h, has S(h) nodes, S(h) = S(h − 1) + S(h − 2) + 1.

  1. Assume A is the lowest node violating valance property. Assuming that A is left heavy, B is A's left child

    • B is balanced / left heavy: right rotate A

    • B is right heavy: right rotate B then right rotate A.

  2. Deletion: walk up the tree and rotate every unbalanced node.

If the number of keys in heavier sub-tree is at most twice the number of keys in lighter sub-tree, the tree is balanced but no height-balanced.

Tree Operation conclusion

  1. modifying:
    • insert: O(h)
    • delete: O(h)
  2. Query Operations:
    • search: O(h)
    • predecessor / successor: O(h)
    • findMax / findMin: O(h)
    • Traversal: O(n)

Interval Queries

search:

when we walk down the nodes to(17,19), the information that we need are: 17(left interval), 25(max = left subtree)

  • Case 1: x > 25 (max of left subtree):

    Go right. (the node is either in right tree or not in tree!)

  • Case2: 17 < x <= 25

    Go left. (there must be atleast one node that contains x!)

  • Case3: x < 17

    Go left. (there is 0 possibility that the right subtree contains x!)

In three cases we are safe to go (the direction we go contains the key / the direction we don't go does not contain key)

insertion & rotation

easy to maintain

Others

[U] Extension: List all intervals that overlap with point?

Orthogonal Range Searching

Introduction

Are there any good restaurants within one block of me - search for the points in a given box

Sample: One-Dimension

Strategy
  1. Use a binary search tree and store all the points in the leaves of a tree. Each internal node stores the MAX of any leaf in the left sub-tree.

  1. Do Search(10, 50): (Time complexity: O(log(n) + k), where k is the number of items)

    • Find Split Node: the highest node that search includes both left and right subtrees(in this case, 25) O(logn)
    • Do left and right traversal on the left and right tree. O(k)
    • Left Traversal: The search interval for a left-traversal at node v includes the maximum item in the left subtree of v.

  2. BuildTree time complexity : O(n log n) ([U] why?)

  3. Dynamic updates: Rotation ([U] how?)

Two-Dimension

  1. Idea:
  • 1: Create a 1d-range-tree on the x-coords.
  • 2: Store a y-tree at each x-node containing all the points in the sub-tree.

[U] don't quite understand.

k-d Tree

space partitioning data structure for organising points into a 𝑘-dimensional space

B-trees

(a, b) trees

Rules

  • 2 ≤ a ≤ (b + 1)/2, where a and b refers to the minimum and maximum number of children in a internal node(not root / leaf).

  • every node should follow that: numberOfChild = numberOfKey + 1. For a node with k keys (v1,v2,...,vk), the subtrees(t1,t2,...,tk+1) should follow that ： First child t1 has key range ≤ v1； Final child tk+1 has key range > vk； All other children ti where i ∈ [2, k] has key range (vi−1, vi)

  • All leaves should have the same depth.

Manipulation

  1. Is ab tree balanced?

  2. Insertion:

Solution 1: (1) At first put the element in its correct place in the node (which is in sorted order)

  1. If the node overflows, then find the median ( = if is even and if is odd ) key in that node.

  2. Put the median key in the parent and split the current node

Solution 2: Proactive Approach

Preemptively split a node whe it is full so that it does not overflow in the future.

  1. Deletion:
  1. Case 1: Problem with Orphan Children.
In this case, we replace the deleted node either with the predecessor or successor key
  1. Case 2: Problem with downflow

If at least one sibling has at least a keys, then donate the key to the other sibling

If none have enough keys, then merge the two siblings and the parent together.

B-trees

B-trees are simply (B, 2B)-trees. That is to say, they are a subcategory of (a, b)-trees such that a = B and b = 2B.

  1. Cost

  • All the costs of searching a key list / splitting a node / merging / sharing B-tree nodes are O(1)!

  • The cost of searching a B-tree / inserting / deleting in a B tree is O(loga n)!

Others

  1. log2n = O( log10n ) Correct! We can easily switch between bases by multiplying by a constant.

Lecture 12 Hashing

Basic Concepts

  1. Difference with trees: no comparison between keys. [U] Why do you need O(n^2) time to sort in symbol table

  2. For java.util.Map<Key, Value>

    • No duplicates are allowed

    • Keys are immutable

  1. How does java implement hashFunctions?
  • hashCode should be:

  1. reflexive, symmetric, transitive

  2. consistent(always return the same answer)

  3. simultanious with equals method! (returns the same hashcode if and only if two objects are the same!)

  1. Two Good properties of a good Hash Function:

    • For every bucket j, there is some i such that h(key, i) = j. (make sure that you make use of all the space)

    • Simple Uniform Hashing Assumption: Every key is equally likely to be mapped to every permutation, independent of every other key. [U] wut? Why does linear probing violate the rule

Coping with Collision

Concept

Collision: h(k1) = h(k2), then k1 and k2 collide, where h is a hash function that takes in a key(e.g. "Hello") and returns the index where it should be stored in the array.

Chaining

  1. Why don't we just insert each key into a random bucket?

    • search would be slow!

    • Simple Uniform Hasing Assumption: every key is equally likely to map to every bucket.

      • What is the maximum chain length with SUHA? - O(log(n)) ([U] Why?)

      • To minimize the maximum chain length, we use two function h1 and h2. store the k in the bucket with a smaller chain. This would reduce the maximum chain length to O(log log n)

Open Addressing

  1. Concept: find the next bucket! one itme per slot and try to fill in all buckets.

  2. Idea: probe a sequence of buckets until you find an empty one.

    • h(key, i) where i is the time that you are trying (to find the slot), Do note that it may not be that h(key, i) = h(key, i - 1) + 1 and the "sequence" of objects may be arranged in random order.

  3. Problem: Deletion! If I simply set the box to null, when I try to search after deletion, I would stop at the deleted box and not search for any element after that(we assume that search stops as the first null, since we only stores elements in order). To solve that, we use DELETED for deleted elements instead of null.

  4. Linear probing:

    • Clusters! There are clusters and there is a higher probability for the next h(k) to hit the cluster

    • In practice, however, linear probing is faster. This is because caching!

  5. Double Hashing!

    • Use two hash functions f, g. \[h(k,i) = f(k) + i * g(k)\] ([U] Claim: if g(k) is relatively prime to m, then h(k,i) hits all buckets. L15)

  6. Performance(assuming uniform hashing) ([U] why do you need this? Proof? L15):

if \[\alpha = n/m\], then \[Cost = \frac {1}{1 - \alpha}\]

So performancee degrades badly as \(\alpha\) -> 1

Resizing

Concept

  1. I don't know how many elements are going to be inserted! - Grow the table!

  2. Idea: Implement a new slot m2 and a new hash function. Scan the items in m1 (old hash table) and insert them in a new hash table - O(n)

How fast to grow the hash table?

  1. m = m + 1: O(n^2); m = 2m: O(n); m = m^2: O(n^2)

  2. Shrinking and growing: if(n === m), then m = 2m. if(n < m / 4), then m = m /2

    • Claim: Everytime you double a table of size m, at least m / 2 new items were added.

    • Everytime you shrink a table of size m, at least m / 4 items were deleted.

  3. Technique of analyzing "average cost" (when some operations are cheap and some are expensive): Operation has amortized cost T(n) if for every integer k, the cost of k operations is <= kT(n)

    • e.g. For a list of insertions, the 5 insertions cost 5,5,5,13,7 respectively. The amortized cost = 7, where 5 < 7; 5 + 5 < 7 * 2; 5 + 5 + 5 < 7 * 3...etc

    • We claim that in this sense, the insert operation of a hash function has amortized cost O(1) [U] Proof

  4. [U] What if we don't want to be the person who triggers the 2 hour hash table incrementing?

Lecture 14: Heaps

Priority Queue

Heaps

Two properties

  1. Heap Ordering: priority[parent] >= priority[child] (different from a binary search tree!)

  2. Complete Binary Tree:

    • Every level is full, except the last

    • All nodes are as far left as possible

Features

  1. The maimum hbeight of a heap with n elements: floor(log n)

  2. Storing: we store the tree in an array in BFS order. In this case, it is easier to access the last node and the root node.

  • How do we find the parent of a node? Since the tree is a Complete Binary Tree, the parent index would be: (k-1) / 2 !

Operations

  1. Insert:

    • Step one: add the new leaf to the left most of the last level

    • bubble up: swap the leaf that doesn't satify the Heap Ordering property with the parent. [U] Why would this work

  2. Increase Key: increase a key from e.g. 5 -> 25.

    • bubble up 25.

  3. Decrease Key : 28 -> 4

  • We bubble down with the child with the bigger one (22)

  1. Delete:

    • swap the node with the last node

    • delete the node

    • bubble down the last node

  2. extractMax: output the max and delete it.

Performancee:

All these operations are O(log n)!

HeapSort

  1. Thought1: Unsorted List -> Heap -> Sorted List

    • Procedure: Keep doing extractMax!

    • Time complexity: O(n log n). Faster than Mergesort and slower than Quicksort.

    • Unstable [U]

    • Ternary (3-way) HeapSort is a little faster

  2. Thought 2: Heapify!

    • Procedure: view the array as a complete tree. Do recursion!

      • Base case: Each leaf node is a heap

      • Heapify upwards where each left and right are heaps.(by bubble down)

    • Code:

    • Cost:

    \[\sum\limits_{h = 0}^{log(n)} {\frac {n}{2^h} O(h)} = O(n)\]

    • [U] wait so why doesn't this count? Or why is heapsort consideered O(n log n)? Because to convert a heap into an array you still need O(n log n) ？

Sets

[U] I did not understand a single word he said

Graph

Basic

Terminology

  1. Nodes: At least one; Edges: Each Edge connect two nodes(In hypergraph, each edge connects >=2 nodes in the graph)

  2. \[Graph G = <V,E>\], where V is a set of nodes, E is set of edges

  3. Simple path: set of edges connecting two nodes, which intersects eaach node at most once

    • Connected: all nodes is connected by a path

    • Cycle: where the last and first node are the same(not actually a path)

  4. Degree: number of adjacent edges.

  5. Diameter: Maximum distance between two nodes following the

  6. Cut of agraph is a partition of the vertices into two disjoint subssets.

    • Edge across the cut is the edge that has one node in each of the subsets

shortest path

  1. Directed Path: Each edge is directed

    • In-degree and Out-degree

  2. Weight: the cost to travel from v1 to v2 is w[v1][v2]. It can be a negative number

Different Kinds of Graphs

  1. Unrooted Tree: Connected graph with no cycles. Forest if not connected

  2. Star: one central node that has connection to all other nodes ([Do the foot of the stars need to be connected?])

  3. Clique(Complete Graph): All pairs are connected by edges(dimaeter: 1)

  4. Cycle: a circle.

  5. Bipartite: There exists a partition, where the edges only go from one edge to another. (Method to judge whether one graph is a bipartite: choose a random node and color it red, color the neighbours blue...etc)

  6. Planar graph: You can draw the graph in a way such that no edges intercross each other

Storing Data

  1. Adjacency Matrix : Graph represented as A[v][w] = 1 iff there is an edge between v and w. Neat property: A^2 = if there is length 2 paths([U] How does this work)

  2. Memory usage for graph G = (V, E)

    • Adjacency List: O(V+E)

    • Adjacency Matrix: O(\(V^2\))

    • if a graph is dense ( |E| = O(\(V^2\)) ) use adjacency Matrix

BFS and DFS

Concept

  1. Level: the 1st level consists of nodes that have path of length 1. The 2nd level consists of nodes that have path of length 2 but do not have path of length 1.

BFS

  1. Steps:

    • Build levels.(calculate level[i] from level[i - 1], skip already visited nodes)
  1. Complexity: O(V + E). Each vertex is added once and each edge is added once. (Do note the difference between E-edges and number of neighbours in each vertex here.)

DFS

Shortest Paths

  1. Key idea: triangle inequality \[cost[s][c] <= cost[s][a] + cost[a][c]\]

Shortest Path from s to every node

Bellman-Ford

Code
Proof

Suppose there is no negative-weight cylce ([S] What about those that don't satify? - Then it makes no sense calculating the shortest path, just keep going in circles![^4]) [^4]: In fact, you can use Bellman-Ford to try finding a negative weight cycle - just try to see whether edge is relaxed after n-1 iterations!

  1. Invariant: After j iterations(the outer loop), for any j-hop node u, cost[u] stores the correct answer. j-hop node refers to the node u where on the shortest path from s to u, there are j edges.

  2. Proof by Induction

    • Base case: After 1st iteration, all 1-hop nodes stored the correct value \[cost[u] = weight(s,u)\]

    • If \[cost[hop_{j - 1}] = ultimate\_value\] after one iteration, the edge connecting (j-1)hop nodes and j-hop nodes are relaxed. Thus \[cost[hop_j] = ultimate\_value\]

    • Since all nodes in Graph are at most n-1 hop nodes (since there is no cylce in the ultimate shortest path)

  3. Performance: O(VE). If weight of all edges are the same, then just use BFS*

Dijkstra

Basic Idea

  1. Steps: Maintain a priority queue, which stores the keys and is prioritized with the current estimated dist[k]. Maintain an array vis[], where vis[k] = true if the node k is visited, or dist[k] stores the correct answer.

    • Initialize: change all nodes to be unvisited and all \(dist[k] = infinity\). Put the starting node into the priority queue and change dist[s] = 0.

    • Loop: For each loop, extract the minimum element in priority queue, lets say m. mark m as visited(we'll explain why dist[m] stores the correct answer in a second)

    • Relax all edges connected to m. Put the nodes which are not visited in the queue, update the nodes that are in the queue

    • Repeat the Loop step.

[U] How did they come up with such a genius idea! Rethink about the thinking process.

Proof

Imagine all the nodes on the graph to be of three categories: visited nodes(nodes that already have the currect distance stored), fringe nodes (unvisited nodes that have at least one edge that are connected to visited nodes), and unknown nodes (these are nodes whose information are completely unknown and don't have any edge connected to the visited nodes). As shown in the picture below.

Before the proof, there are four properties that a graph would hold.

  1. Any subpath of a shortest path is a shortest path. (Proof by contradiction)

  2. All fringe nodes are in the priority queue.

  3. All shortest paths that ends with an unvisited node would involve at least one visited node and one fringe node.

  4. At anytime, for any node v, \[curr\_dist[v] >= dist[v]\]

Now, we proof by induction: If at anytime, suppose for the visited elements, all distance stored are correct, then the next element that we delete in the priority queue would store the correct dist[m].

We will prove this by contradiction:

  • Suppose we delete the minimum element from the priority queue, m, the dist[m] stores the wrong value. However, the "shortest path" that involves m is the orange path shown in the picture.

  • By property(3), suppose the first fringe node that the shortest path involves is w. Since m is removed from priority queue(with property(2)), \[curr\_dist[m] > curr\_dist[w]\tag{1}\]

  • However, since m is in the shortest path to w([U] Is this true? does curr_dist[m] really stores the correct value?), \[curr\_dist[m] = dist[m]\tag{2}\], by property(1), \[dist[m] > curr\_dist[w]\tag{2.5}\]. Since we know that m is in the shortest path to w, we say \[dist[m] <= dist[w]\tag{3}\], we conclude that \[dist[w] >= curr\_dist[w] \tag{4}\], which is contradictory to property(4).

The proof only works for weightsthat are all positive. If there is negative weight edges, formula(3) will not hold. This is different from Bellman-Ford Algorithm

Performance Analysis

Time Complexity: we insert / deletMin for |V| times each, since every node is added to the priority queue oncec. We decreaseKey at most |E| times. Since priority queue operations are O(log V) \[runtime = O((V+E)logV) = O(E log V)\]

  • Priority Queue by AVL tree(with Hash Table): O((V + E) log V) = O(E log V)

    • insert: O(log n)

    • deleteMin(): O(log n)

    • decreaseKey(key, priority): O(log n)

    • contains(key): O(1)

  • Performance iseven vetter if use d-way Heap or Fibonacci Heap(?)

Special Cases

  1. For Trees, we can relax in the order of BFS/DFS!

Topological Sort

Directed Acyclic Graph(e.g. scheduling cooking steps!):

  • Definition: graphs that has no cycles.

  • Topological order: there exists(more than one) sequential total ordering of all nodes, such that when I travel the nodes in that order, I would not disobey the order. [U] Why

  • We use post-order DFS!

    • Why don't we use BFS?[U]

    • Why don't we use pre-order DFS?([U] I mean I can understand that it doesn't work but what's the key point of determining which algorithm to use here?)

  • Time Complexity: O(V+E)

  • Alternative Solution

    • Add all nodes in Graph that has no incoming edges to the topo-order, remove all the edges adjacent to these nodes, remove the nodes, repeat.

    • O(V + E)([U]Why? No EXPLAINATION?)

  • After we Topological Sort the problem, we just relax the nodes in topological order. ([U] How exactly do you implement this, it's quite crucial for proving the correctness)

Weight Union

  1. Functionalities

    • Union(i, j)

    • isConnected(i, j): return whether i and j are in the same union

    • Union-Split-Find: for weight unions that implements this functionality, check THIS

  2. Idea: store data in a tree structure. Each tree is a union. When union i, j, find the root of i and j and merge the two trees.

    • Find(i) : find the root of i

    • Merge(rt1, rt2): union the two trees. We merge trees by setting the parent of the root of the smaller tree to be the root of the other tree.

  3. Performance

    • isConnected(i, j) and Union(i, j) : O(h) / O(log n)

    • Proof that h = O(log n) : Proof that for any tree of height h has at least \(2^k\) nodes. Proof this by induction: suppose for height k trees this is true, proof that for height k+1 trees this is true.

    • improvement: set parent of every node that is traversed to be the root node (flattens the tree - path compression!)

    • Starting from empty, any sequence of m union/find operations on n objects takes: O(n + mα(m, n)time., where α is the inverse arckermann function ([U] ? Proof and some knowledge about the function? Tarjan 1975)

  4. Application

    • Binomial tree

Minimum Spanning Trees

Basics

  1. Spanning Trees: In a weighted, undirected graphs([U] Why is it more complicated with directed graphs), spanning tree is an acyclic subset of the edges that connects all nodes.

  2. Minimum Spanning tree: a subset of a undirected weighted graph which connects all nodes and has the least total weight.

Properties

Assumption: For simplification, assume all edge weights are distinct.

  1. No cycles are in the MST.

  2. Cut Property: if you cut an edge on a minimum spanning tree, then the rest two components T1 and T2, would still be the MST for the respective nodes.(However, T1 MST + T2 MST + Minimum edge != (T1 + T2)MST)

  3. Cycle Property: For any cycle on the original graph, the maximum weigt edge is not in the MST.

    • Proof: Proof by contradiction - If the maximum weight edge is in the MST. By property 1, at least 1 edge of the cycle is not in the MST, lets all these edges Enot. By property 2, if we cut the maximum weight edge, the rest of the two parts are still MSTs, then if we add one edge that connects the two parts, it would have a smaller weight. Now we just need to proof that there is at least one Enot that connects the two parts. ([U] how do you prove this? By cycle property?)

  4. For every partition of the nodes, the minimum weight edge across the cut is in the MST. (However, it might not be the only one!)

    • For every vertex, the minium outgoing edge is always part of the MST.

Generic MST Algorithm

  1. Apply the red rule or vlue rule to an arbitrary edge until no more edges can be colored.

    • Red Rule: If C isa cycle with no red arcs, then color the max-weight edge in C red. (Property 2)

    • Blue Rule: If D is a cut with no blue arcs, then color the min-weight edge in D blue. (Property 4)

  2. Proof of the greedy algorithm:

    • On termination, every cycle has a red edge, and the blue edges form a tree(if not, then there is a cut with no blue edge). Every edge is colored. ([U]Why, can't there be an eedge that neither satisfy the red rule, nor satisfy the blue rule? )

MST Algorithms

Prim

  1. Basic Idea:

    • S: set of nodes connected by blue edges.

    • Repeat: Identify the cut: {S, V - S}

    • Find minimum weight edge on cut (Priority queue)

    • Add new node to S

[U] In the code for prim, why should the priority queue keep track of the nodes rather than simply keep track of the weights?

Kruskal

  1. Basic idea:

    • sort edges by weight from smallest to biggest.

    • Consider edges in ascending order:

      • If both endpoints are in the same blue tree, then color the edge red. (Must be the heaviest edge in the loop!)

      • else, color the edge blue.

Variations of MST

  1. What if all edges have the same weight?

    • DFS / BFS!

  2. What if all edges have weight of {1...10} ?

    • In kruskal algorithm, we no longer need to sort the edges, we just use array of size 10. (Why does thi make time complexity \(O(\alpha{E})\))

    • In Prim's algorithm, we use array of size 10 as the priority queue. Where slot A[j] stores a linked list of nodes of weight j. The time complexity would be \(O(E)\)([U] Go through thi).

[U] Why would this fail for Dijkstra's Algorithm?

  1. Directed Minimum Spanning Trees

    • Special Case: For a directed acyclic graph with one "root", We can generate MST by adding minimum weight incoming edge for every node except the root([U] Why?)

  2. Maximum Spanning Trees:

    • Only relative weight matters.

    • multiply each edge by -1 (why does this work)

Dynamic Programming

Basic

  1. Optimal substructures

  2. Overlapping sub-problems: smaller problems are used to solve multiple different bigger problems.

  3. Check the Floyd Warshall Problem

Graph Summary

[U] Where should I put Topological sort, under shortest paths or parrallel? Should it be named Topological sort or DAG?

Problems (in tutorials)

Mountain stack

A mountains consists of n hills, aligned linearly. Each hill i, stands at a height of a[i]. Standing at hill i, you’ll see a point j kilometers away if and only if all hills from i−j to i are no taller than a[i] meters. For each hill i, determine the maximum distance you can see from it(distance is "-1" if no hill blocks your sight). (Hint: try using a stack to achieve O(n) time complexity)

Answer: Use a monotonic decreasing stack:

  • For every element at index i in the array:

[U] This is somehow incorrect. But the idea is similar. pls correct this.

How to come up with this: Gather only the information you need! Information for any hill i that is lower than another hill j where j > i can be eliminated!

Chicken Rice Competetion :

  • You have in front of you n plates of chicken rice, each plate contains n bites of chicken rice. Supppose you want to find the median chicken rice. Design an algorithm to maximize the amount of remaining chicken rice on the median plate.
  • Solution 1: Run QuickSelect: on one level of QuickSelect, the expectation of time of bitten on any plate is (1 / n) * (n - 1) + (1 - 1 / n) * 1 <= 2. Thus the expected consumption of chicken rice is O(2 log n) bites.
  • Solution 2: Inserting each plate into an AVL tree takes O(log n) comparisons. (However, if median is the root of AVL tree, there would be O(n) moves!)
  • In fact, using a randomized algorithm, this can be solved with only O(log log(n)) bites to the median plate, but that is much, much more complicated [U]

Weight Selection(Eonomic Research):

  • You are an economist doing a research on the wealth of different generations. Each row of data you've gained consists of a unique identifier, an age, and a number that represents their amount of wealth. Your goal is to divide the dataset into “equi-wealth” age ranges. That is, given a parameter k, you should produce k different age ranges A1, A2, ..., Ak with the following properties:
    1. All the ages of people in set \(A_j\) should be <= the ages of people in \(A_{j+1}\).
    2. The sum of wealth in each set should be (roughly) the same (tolerating rounding errors if k does not divide the total wealth, or exact equality is not attainable).

You should assume that the given k is relatively small while the dataset is very large . (i.e. O(nk) < O(n log n))

Design the most efficient algorithm you can to solve this problem. Optionally, consider the case where rows can consist of the same ages, versus the case where rows have unique ages. - Solution: a weight selection problem. We are asking to find the 1/k,2/k,...,k−1/k order statistics of the weighted sum 1. Finding a single Weight Order Statistic. - First, use QuickSelect to find the median aged dataset, and then partition around the median - sum the totals on the left and right halves. (DO the sum while partitioning to save time!) If target weight > left sum, subtract left sum weight from your target weight and search on right half...etc. This is O(n) time. 2. repeat for the k - 1 targets and Do one final partitioning to build your output lists([U] why is this nessary) This is O(nk) time.

  • Solution 2: Instead of finding the median, we use a random pivot. Each time we partition, we keep track of the sum of the wealth to the left of the selected pivots! We do Quickselect O(k) times and each selection takes O(n) times. ([U] Needs further explanation, see tutorial slides w6 "better solution") [U] Why is this better than Solution 1?

  • Solution 3: Find k order statistics all at once! When random pivot was selected, say its 2 * \(\bar{w}\), then we recurse on the left to find the 1st order-statistics and recurse on the right to find the 3rd order-statistics! In this case, we get a rough overall runtime of O(n log k) ([U] Needs further explanation, see tutorial slides w6 "better solution")

  • [U] How about naiive solution?

Height of Binary Tree After Subtree Removal

  • Description: You are given a binary tree with n nodes. You are given m queries, where queries[i] asks the height of the tree after deletion of the subtree containing the ith node. What is the optimized solution?

  • Solution 1 : Build a tree. for each query, walk up the tree and try to update the node's height. O(log n * m) ([U] Why does the slides say its O(n * m)?)

  • Solution 2: Depth of a node refers to the number of edges in the simple path from root to the node

    1. preprocess the tree to get its depth using DFS and height using in-order-traversal.
    2. For ith query, we look at all the nodes with the same depth of ith node and find the maximum height among them. The answer to the query would be node[i].depth + max_height.
    3. SO how do we group the nodes of the same depth? We do this by iterating the nodes in in-order-traversal([U] wut?). For each group of nodes in the same depth, we only require the maximum height and second maximum height. To do this we need O(n) time([U] wut?).
    4. In this algorithm, we only need O(1) for each query. The overal time complexity would be O(n + m)

[U] There is another method based on Euler Tour Tree (which is out of syllabus of CS2040S). You may find more details about the data structure on

https://courses.csail.mit.edu/6.851/spring07/scribe/lec05.pdf and http://web.stanford.edu/class/archive/cs/cs166/cs166.1166/lectures/17/Small17.pdf.

The data structure provide a neat alternative solution and support for deleting multiple subtrees.

Safely Evaluating average pay!

  1. Question 1
  • Come up with an algorithm that will allow the students to determine the average pay of the group without revealing their own salaries to any of the other members in the group
  • Solution 1: Everyone pick a random number and add it to there salery. 1st person passes the sum to the 2nd person, the 2nd person add their sum to the number the 1st one passed ... Until everyone's sum adds up. Then they deduct the number one by one.
  1. Extention:
  • what happens if k members of the group decide to collude and share information. Is your algorithm still able to preserve the privacy of all the members, or could the salary for some of the members be leaked
  • Solution 1 will not work! the (i-1)th and (i+1)th person can easily calculate the ith people's salery! ([U] is there a way to manipulate the order of adding and subtracting such that there is no way they can do that?)
  • Solution 2: Divide the salery into n pieces and each person give the ith piece of their salery to the ith people! Even if everyone in the house except for the kth person cheat and try to calculate kth person's salery, they cannot do so! ([U] How did you come up with that!)
  • Way to come up with a good algorithm: [U] ?
    1. How you (re)define the problem
    2. Determines what are the metrics of success
    3. How to iterate a solution?

Pancake flipping!

  1. Description: Try to serve pancakes a stack sorted by size. You can only flip the pancakes at the top of the pancake stack.

  2. Show that any pancake flipping algorithm requires at least n flips. What do we call such properties of the problem? [U] Check out recitation 2

  • A lower bound of the algorithm!
  • Consider a possible worst case! Why is this the worst case? [U] Worst case should differ for different algorithms?
    • Feature 1: if two consecutive pancakes in the sorted pancake stack are together in the unsorted stack then we can treat those pancakes as one pancake either with burnt side up or down based on their order. This means that if 2 4 are adjacent in the original pancake, we must use at least one flip between 2 and 4 to seperate them!
    • Feature 2: If the largest pancake is at the bottom we can simply ignore it.
    • By Feature 1, we need at least n - 1 flips. By feature 2, we need at least 1 additional flip to seperate the bottom one and the table! Thus the lower bound is at least n flip.

[U] Check out recitation 2 on more about pancake flipping.

DNA Swapping

  1. Description
  • We are doing sorting when only calculating the cst of swapping subarrays. The cost of swapping subarrays of length k would cost O(k).
  1. What if we are sorting an array of two element? - Do mergesort!
  2. What if the input string comprises of arbitrary characters without duplicates.
  • Wait, we can do this using the solution in 2! partition is the same as sorting an array of two kinds of elements! The time complexity is T(n) = 2 * T(n / 2) + O(n log n). [U]

Random Permutation

  1. Given an array of n items, we need to return a random permutation of the given array.

  2. How do we evaluate the performance of a random permutation algorithm!

  • The possibility of each permuatation to appear in the array should be n!

  1. Consider the Fisher-Yates / Knuth Shuffle algorithm: Time Complexity O(n) and Space complexity O(1)! [U] why is space complexity O(1)

  2. Solution 2: assign a random number to each element and sort it!

  • Question: what if we meet duplicate numbers?
  • If the sorting is stable, simply treat the earlier occurence to be the smaller one([U] Is this still random? )
  • If not, the sorting would not be stable!

Binary Counter Amortized Cost

  1. Suppose we have a binary counter, after n increments, what is the amortized cost?

  2. Simple answer: O(log n). But this is not the case!

  3. Closer look: 2! [U] Seems kind of unclear

    • Observation: each increament would only change one 0 to 1.

    • Suppose each bit is a bank. When we change from 0 to 1, add 1 dollar, when we change from 1 to 0, pay 1 dollar.

[U] Check the ppt

Scheduling

  1. Suppose we have a list of tasks, some of the has constraints like A has to be done at least 10 minitues before B, C must be done after D, E must be done at most 20 minutes after F...etc

  2. Think of this as a DAG problem! Imagine the tasks as nodes and the edges as constraints of the nodes, dist[u] means the shortest time needed to complete u.

    • For constraint: A has to be done at least 10 minutes before B. this means \(dist[A] + 10 <= dist[B]\) since in graphs we only have \[dist[u] <= dist[v] + weight[u,v]\], we transform the constraint to be \[dist[A] <= dist[B] - 10\], therefore, the edge between A and B would be \(-10\).

    • Now, we add Starting point S with edges of weight 0 connected to all nodes and run Bellman-Ford on S.

    • Time Complexity: \(O(n * m)\), where n, m is the number of tasks and constraints respectfully. ([U] Why don't we use topological sort?)

Puzzles:

  1. [U]Sicherman Dice

  2. [U]Puzzle of the Week: Bubbly (Courtesy: MIT Puzzle Hunt, 2019)

  3. [U]Treasure Island:

You choose a set of keys.

You can test whether all the good keys are in the set (and possibly

some extra keys). If even one bad key is in the set, then it fails.

Goal: identify exactly the good keys.

  1. PotW: Gambling for Profit? (Lecture 07 p121)

  2. Prisoners! (L10)

** Updated Until:

Lecture 11 p103

tut 2

[U] optional questions in tutorials.

[U] Check the open resourse

[U] Check this out: visualAlgo

[U] HashTable - Tutorial Slides W6 - problem 2 -credit CS2109S

[U] Optional problems in tuts and recs

There are some useful advice on midterms in Week 6 tut materal

entry.title: CS2030S Lecture Notes
entry.content:
CS2030S lecture notes.

Java

  1. Java Compiler: compiled into bytecode.(.java-> javac -> .class); interpreted and compiled on the fly by JVM into machine code.(java .class) / be interpreted by the Java interpreter.

  2. Types:

  • statically typed language: declare every variable we use in the program and specify its type(compile-time type).
  • Primitive: numeric & boolean values byte <: short <: int <: long <: float <: double; char <: int
  • Reference Types: stores only the reference to the value
  • widening type conversion: long i = int(j); narrowing type conversion: type casting
  1. Principals:
  • encapsulation: keeping all the data and functions operating on the data related to a composite data type together within an abstraction barrier.
  • "Tell, Don't Ask" principle. The client should tell a Circle object what to do , instead of asking "What is your radius?" to get the value of a field
  • composition: combining classes
  • Composition : has-a relationship;Inheritance : is-a relationship.
  • polymorphism: method invoked is decided during run-time(dynamic binding/dispatch) - Class Methods do not support dynamic binding. At compile time, determines the meth od descriptor(including return type) of the method invoked, using the compile-time type of the target. At runtime, the method descriptor is retrieved and the run-tim type is determined
  • Inheritance: subtypes can only access public fields/methods
  • Liskov Substitution Principle: A subclass should not break the expectations set by the superclass

  1. package: import java.lang.Math; return Math.PI * this.r * this.r;

  2. The main method

  3. aliasing: sharing references. Avoid that.e.g.:

  4. Stack and Heap

  • heap : objects - whenever you use the keyword new
  • Stack: variables in method call frames(main/moveTo...)
  • non-static methods, including constructors, have this
  • Initialization: object- null; Primitive - zero(uninitialized - ∅)

  1. Representing a method: Object::equals(Object) - a method in the class Object

  2. Method override and overload:

  • @Override: Acts as a hint to the compiler. Use super to call the overriden method.

  • method overloading ： differing method signature.

  1. obj instanceof Circle (runtime)

  2. final

  • final class Circle{} To prevent being inhereited
  • public final boolean contains(Point p) To prevent being overriden
  1. abstract: cannot be instantiated - concrete class
  2. Interface:
  • across different class hierarchies.(public abstract by default.) An interface is a type(a type can have multiple supertypes.e.g. a class can be subype of multiple interface)
  • casting unrelated interface is allowed. E.g.: class A, Interface B, class C extends A implements B. There is possiblity that C(subclass of A) can be casted on B. In this situation A = B compiles. default implementation: default (impure interfaces)
  1. Wrapper Classes : primitive wrapper class objects are immutable
  2. variance of types:
    • covariant : S<:T -> C(S) <: C(T); contravariant;invariant: neither Arrays of primitive types are invariant
  3. Exceptions

  • instances that are a subtype of the Exception. Follows the hierarchy

  • Checked: programmer's errors. Would not happen if perfect code was written;

  • Unchecked:InvalidInputException, subclass of RuntimeException

  1. only reference types can be used as type arguments. Comparable<T> dictates the implementation of int compareTo(T t)

  • Note that we use extends for interface bounding

  • the constructor for a generic class is still declared as Pair (without the type parameters).

  • Once a generic type is instantiated, it is called a parameterized type

  1. Type erasure: after type checking, erase the type parameters and type arguments during compilation(to Object). If the bounded, replaced by the bounds. After type erasure, we do explicit typecast on an object.

  • arrays are reifiable type(gets full type information during run-time); but generics are not due to type erasure

  • Actually, generic array declaration is fine but generic array instantiation is not.

  1. ArrayList: a generic class,checks appropriate types during compile time. Note that it is invariant.

  2. @SuppressWarning to the most limited scope

    raw types: only used in instance of

  3. Wild Cards:

  • public void copyTo(Seq<? super T> dest) super is a contravariance type

  • PECS: Producer Extends Consumer Super

  • Seq<Object> is NOT supertype of all Seq<T> due to generics being invariant.

  • Seq<?> is a sequence of objects of some specific, but unknown type;

  • Seq<Object> is a sequence of Object instances, with type checking by the compiler;(Note that generics are invariants so Seq<Object> !>: Seq<Integer>)

  • Seq is a sequence of Object instanc es, without type checking.

  1. Type Infering: T <: Type2, then T is inferred as Type2

  2. Immutability:

  • the instance cannot have any visible changes outside its abstraction barrier.

  • To make a method in a class immutable, it is recommended to declare the class as immutable final too as subclasses can override the method([S]why not declare the method as final? - the final keyword prevents assigning new values to the field, but if the field is referencing a mutable object, it is possible to mutate it. So we also need to ensure that the fields themselves are immutable.)

  • advatanges: allow sharing of reference between objects.

  1. Nested Classes

  • Inner class: non-static nested class

  • When we call this.x in a nested class B in class A, this refers to instance field in B rather than A. To call the x field in A, we use A.this.x. This is called a \(qualified\) this.

  • if a nested class is private, we cannot access an instance of nested class outside the class. However, for nested class B in class A, if there is a method in A B getB() {return new B();}, we can return an instance of B outside A. So we can do: Object b = a.getB();, but we cannot do A.B b = a.getB()

  • However, as long as the nested class B does not expose any detail of class A, it is enouraged to declare B as a public type. Then we can use A.B b = a.new B();

  • local class: non-static nested class in a method. A local class makes a copy of local variables inside itself. This is called \(capturing\).

  • Java only allows local classes to access variables that are effectively final. They cannot be reassigned after initializstion. However, if the variables are reference type, they cannot be reassigned but can be mutated. See the below example. The \(capturing\) is done after the instantiation of a nested class - the list is sorted in descendingOrder

  • Anonymous class: An anonymous class is where you only instantiate a local class in a single statement. There is a syntatic sugar:(e.g. the above code)
  1. Pure Functions: deterministic(return same y for same x), referential transparency(same x for same y), no side effects(no printing/exception/changing of other variables etc.).

  • Functional Interface: interface that has a single abstract method

  • lambda: Transformer<Integer, Integer> t = (/*Integer*/ i) -> (i + 1);, where Transformer is a functional Interface. A lambda function is the same as a local class so it captures local variables used. Thus we store the function together ith the enclosing environment - closure.

  • Function Reference: Transformer<Point, Double> t = origin::distanceTo(); which is the same as: t = p -> origin.distanceTo(p)

  • The technique of translating a general n-ary function to a sequence of n unary functions is called currying. e.g. instead of (x,y) -> x+y, we do x -> y -> x + y. These functions are called curried fcuntions. With currying, we can partially apply a function. This is useful if one of the arguments doen't change often/ is too expensive to compute.

  1. Lazy Evaluation:

  • Procrastinate: when we create a lambda, its content is not evaluated. Only when it is called the content is evaluated.

  • Memoize things: Only works when the function is pure!

  1. Stream
  • Limitation: stream can only be operated once. We cannot use terminal operation on a stream multiple times. We cannot iterate a stream multiple times. This would throw IllegalStateException
  1. Monad:

  • The left identity law: Monad.of(x).flatMap(x -> f(x)) must be the same as f(x)

  • The right identity law: monad.flatMap(x -> Monad.of(x)) must be the same as monad

  • associative law: monad.flatMap(x -> f(x)).flatMap(x -> g(x)) must be the same as monad.flatMap(x -> f(x).flatMap(y -> g(y)))

  1. Functor

  • preserving identity: functor.map(x -> x) is the same as functor

  • preserving composition: functor.map(x -> f(x)).map(x -> g(x)) is the same as functor.map(x -> g(f(x)).

  1. Parallel Computing

  • s.parallel().filter(x -> x < 0).sequential().forEach(..); If we call parallel and sequential in the same stream, the last call wins.

  • Conditions of parallelable

    • Interference: one of the operations modify the source stream when the terminal operation is executed.

    • Stateful: the lambda expression's result depends on a state that might change

  • Parallelising Reduce:

    • combiner.apply(identity, i) = i; combiner and accumulator must both be associative and compatible
  1. Side Notes:
  • T... items are called varargs. The methods that use \(varargs\) are called \(variadic method\). Use @SafeVarargs to tell te compiler that we know what we are doing.

Sample Code for PE

  1. remember to make all the numbers encountered static. (private static final int RATE = 22)

  3. Recommended to do(in subclass and superclass)

  4. Vim

  5. Coding Style

  • public protected private abstract default static final transient volatile synchronized native strictfp

[U] find 2 different versions of reduce in stream from java api in here

[U] Read this and this

entry.title: Thoughts for Attending Guest Lecture on Pi
entry.content:
Attending a guest lecture is fun # Thoughts

I never thought about attending guest lectures before. Mainly because all the guest lectures that I attended in high school didn't talk much about "knowledge", but rather about "the history of knowledge" or the guests' experiences. Since we needed to compete for Olympiad and prepare for College Entrance Exams, we didn't have much time for doing "useless" stuff other than studying directly for the exams(at least, that's what I thought, I'll probably write about that as well in another post. If I have time). So I've always been kind of resistant to guest talks.

But then, I've started to change my mind since I came to university in a different country and tried to touch on things that I didn't want to take before(I'll also talk about that in the other post). Plus, I read Cal Newport's How To Win At College (I know this book sounds uitilitarian but it's actually good), in which he suggested listening to guest lectures twice a week. So I've decided to give it a try on a Maths Talks, since I want to know whether my interest actually lies in this realm.

The talk was given by prof Chan Heng Huat, from the Department of Mathematics in NUS. There were also several other profs who attended the meeting such as my prof from GEA1000N (What a surprise that I would be seeing him tmr) and profs from non-mathematics departments. Apart from this, several kids were also there in the talk, listening carefully and silently. That actually surprised me and I was even kind of jealous of those kids who get the chance to get exposed to these interesting talks when they were young(maybe one of them would be the next Ramanujan, who knows!).

Prof Chan mainly talked about the progress and various kinds of techniques or thoughts that were related to apporximating Pi. He used simple language, but dived deep into the thought progress of doing these approximations and gave useful details about the questions that were raised during the talk. The talk lasted for 40 minutes. It wasn't useful in a sense that I would probably forget about all these series and integrations next morning when I wake up. However, I would definetely say that it makes me remember why and how I loved Mathematics when I was young. I can't sum up now about what the talk brought to me or what I've learned in the talk, but I know in my heart that something was changed in my mind. Something was envoked. I cannot tell what it is right now, nor do I know how this would influence my future careers. I can't even say that it is "useful".

But as I am writing this, I remember David Brooks's talk about how everyone has this part of them that does not care about fame or money or tiktok likes, there is a part of us that pursues true happiness and higher realm. I guess I begin to remember how I like Maths as Maths itself, not because "Studying Maths Sounds So Hardcore" or "Maths Is Useful for CS" or anything like that. And I guess that's what guest lectures are for. To guide you to a higher perspective and envoke interest. To put down the utilitarian thoughts and discover something new. To truly enjoy the thing you love and remove the impurities imparted by reality to those things.

I look forward to attending more guest lectures.

This lecture is not a Special one. I wouldn't say that the lecture is the-thought-provoking-one. But as a first guest lecture that I take the initiative to participate, and as a first guest lecture that I attend in university, it does reminds me of something deep in my heart and inspires me to attend future guest lectures.

(Useless) Notes

Here are the notes:(taken real quick so basically not useful lah)

entry.title: Machine Learning Introduction on Coursera
entry.content:
Took the Machine Learning Specialization on Coursera, here's the notes.

Supervised Machine Learning: Regression and Classification

Week 1:Introduction to ML

Supervised VS Unsupervised Learning

  1. Supervised Learning

  • Input - Output prediction

    1. Train your models with x and correct answers Y

    2. Models take brand new X and predict Y

  • Regresion : predict X-Y mappings, where Y has unlimited inifinte possible values

  • CLassification: predict only a small number of outputs Y (categories/classes)

  1. Unsupervised Learning
  • Find something interesting (structure) in unlabeled data. Do not have an aim(e.g. clustering: (google news cluster the related news togethe / grouping different customers); Dimemsionality reduction ; Anomally depression)

Regression model

1. Terminology

  • Training Set: Data set used to train the model
  • x : Feature / input variable
  • y : "target" variable(the correct answer)
  • (x,y) : single training variable
  • (\(x^{(i)}\), \(y^{(i)}\)) : the ith row of training example
  • m : number of training examples
  • f : hypothesis / model
  • \(\hat{y}\) : prediction
  • \(f_{w,b}\)(x) = wx + b w, b: coefficients / weight
  • Univariate Linear Regression: one variable

2. Hands on pactice:

3. Cost Function

  • error: \(\hat{y^(i)}\) - \(y^(i)\)

\[J(w,b) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum\limits_{i = 0}^{m-1} (f_{w,b}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2 \tag{1}\]

[U] Why 1/2m ？

[U] Why is some places in the graph dense while others are loose? Does that imply anything?

Gradient Descent

Implemetation

\[\begin{align*} \text{repeat}&\text{ until convergence:} \; \lbrace \newline \; w &= w - \alpha \frac{\partial J(w,b)}{\partial w} \tag{1} \; \newline b &= b - \alpha \frac{\partial J(w,b)}{\partial b} \newline \rbrace \end{align*}\]

gradient can be defined as:

\[ \begin{align} \frac{\partial J(w,b)}{\partial w} &= \frac{1}{m} \sum\limits_{i = 0}^{m-1} (f_{w,b}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})x^{(i)} \tag{2}\\ \frac{\partial J(w,b)}{\partial b} &= \frac{1}{m} \sum\limits_{i = 0}^{m-1} (f_{w,b}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \tag{3}\\ \end{align} \]

\(\alpha\) : learning rate (determines how big a step would be) (always positive)

Change w by a little step to get closer to the min.

Repeat until convergence (w and b no longer changes much)

That is to say:

if a is too small, gradient descent is slow;

if a is too large, gradient descent may overshoot(never reach minimum), or fail to converge, or even diverge

If we use an appropriate fixed \(\alpha\) , then as we approach local minimum, the steps become smaller because the derivative gets closer to zero

Simultaneous update :

  • Why? Because we want the value of w and b to be the same when calculating the derivative.

Batch Gradient descnet: for each step of the gradient descent, we use all the training examples

Code

Functions to use:

  • compute_gradient implementing equation (2) and (3) above
  • compute_cost implementing \(J(w,b)\)
  • gradient_descent, utilizing compute_gradient and compute_cost

Code Convention: \(\frac{\partial J(w,b)}{\partial b}\) will be dj_db.

The 'quiver plot': The arrow sizes reflect the magnitude of the gradient at that point. The direction and slope of the arrow reflects the ratio of ∂𝐽(𝑤,𝑏)∂𝑤 and ∂𝐽(𝑤,𝑏)∂𝑏

Now, we can implement Gradient Descent:

  • The path makes steady (monotonic) progress toward its goal.
  • initial steps are much larger than the steps near the goal.

Week 2: Regression with multiple input variables

Multiple Linear Regression

Terminologies

  1. \(x_{j}\) : the jth feature
  2. n: number of features
  3. \(\vec{x}^{(i)}\) : feature of the ith example (e.g.: \(\vec{x}^{(i)}\) = [1416 3 2 40])
  4. \(x_{j}^{(i)}\) : value of the feature j of the ith example. (e.g.: \(x_{3}^{(2)}\) = 2)

\[f_{\vec{w}, b} ({\vec{x}}) = \vec{w} * \vec{x} + b = w_{1}x_{1} + w_{2}x_{2} + ... + w_{n} x_{n} + b\] where \[\vec{w} = [w_{1} w_{2} w_{3} ... w_{n}]\]

Vectorization

Arrays in python

In linnear algebra, count from 1; in np, we count from 0

This vectorized implementation using parallel hardware. It is more effecient than using for loop. ([U] Why?)

Indexing and Slicing
Vector Operation
Two Dimensional Arrays

Implementation:

Normal Equation

  • Only for linear regression
  • Solve for w,b without iteration

[U] wut is this?

\[ \begin{align} \frac{\partial J(\mathbf{w},b)}{\partial w_j} &= \frac{1}{m} \sum\limits_{i = 0}^{m-1} (f_{\mathbf{w},b}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})x_{j}^{(i)} \tag{6} \\ \frac{\partial J(\mathbf{w},b)}{\partial b} &= \frac{1}{m} \sum\limits_{i = 0}^{m-1} (f_{\mathbf{w},b}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \tag{7} \end{align} \]

Gradient Descent in Practice

Feature scaling

When the possible values of a feature is large, then the initial w choose would be relatively small. A small change of w might have a huge impact on the cost.

When the value of the features very widely, then it's very likely that the gradient descent is inefficient (similar to the situation when learning rage \(\alpha\) is too large.)

To solve this, we do rescaling.

In rescaling, we aim for -1 <= \(x_j\) <= 1

Example

300<= \(x_1\) <= 2000 , 0 <= \(x_2\) <= 5

  1. Dividing by maximum

    rescale 0.15 <= \(x_1\) <= 1 , 0 <= \(x_1\) <=5

  2. Mean Normalization

    rescale x to -1 <= x <= 1

    \[x_i := \dfrac{x_i - \mu_i}{max - min} \]

    where \(\mu\) is the average

  3. Z-score Normalization

    rescale x to have a mean 0 and standard deviation of 1([U] How?)

    \[x^{(i)}_j = \dfrac{x^{(i)}_j - \mu_j}{\sigma_j} \]

Checking the Convergence of Gradient Descent

Learning Curve

Automatic Convergence Test

Let \(\epsilon\) be ..., if J(w,b) decreases by <= \(\epsilon\) on one iteration, declare convergence

Identifying the Problem

Tips: if the Gradient Descent is not working, change \(\alpha\) to a very small number and check if it is decreasing, else there's something wrong with the code

Values of \(\alpha\) to choose : 0.01 - 0.03 - 0.1 - 0.3 - 1 - 3 ...

Polynomial Regression

The importance of rescaling is shown as \(x, x^2, x^3\) vary quite a lot, and it's important to do rescaling.

In the above example, the weight associated with the \(x^2\)feature is much larger than the other, so \(x^2\) is the most useful in fitting data.

scikit learn

lab for using scikit

Week 3: Classification

Logistic Regression

  1. Problem with linear regression:
  • when new data that is beyong the threshold of the data is added, the function genreated by linear regression shifts, thus making the previous correct prediction change.

  1. Sigmoid Function: \[g(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, 0<g(z)<1\]

  2. Logistic Regression

  • \[\vec{z} = \vec{w} * \vec{x} + b\] \[f_{w,b} = g(\vec{z})\], where \(f_{w,b}\) represents the possibility of the y value to be 1([U] why), or \[f_{\vec{w},b} = P(y = 1 | \vec{x};\vec{w},b)\], which means that the probability is affected by w, b, given the input x
  1. Decision boundary.

  • For a Logistic Regression, lets say we predict y = 1 whenever g(z) > 0.5, vice versa.

  • g(z) > 0.5 iff z > 0. Thus the line where \(z = 0\) is an important line. We call this line decision boundary.

Cost Function for Logistic Regression

Define the loss function to be \(L(predict, actual)\), where \[J(w, b) = \frac{1}{m} \sum{L(f_{w,b}, y[i])}\]

For Logistic Regression, we define the loss function to be:

\[ loss(f_{\vec{w},b}(\vec{x}^{(i)}), y^{(i)}) = \begin{cases} - \log\left(f_{\vec{w},b}\left( \vec{x}^{(i)} \right) \right) & \text{if $y^{(i)}=1$}\\ - \log \left( 1 - f_{\vec{w},b}\left( \vec{x}^{(i)} \right) \right) & \text{if $y^{(i)}=0$} \end{cases} \], or

\[loss(f_{\mathbf{w},b}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}), y^{(i)}) = (-y^{(i)} \log\left(f_{\mathbf{w},b}\left( \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \right) \right) - \left( 1 - y^{(i)}\right) \log \left( 1 - f_{\mathbf{w},b}\left( \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \right) \right)\], where

\[f_{\vec{w},b}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) = g(\vec{w} \cdot\vec{x}^{(i)}+b)\]

This formula is derived from a statistical model called maximum liklihood estimation([U]? )

To check if this make sense, we check

  • whether if the predicted value becomes nearer to the actual value, the loss becomes nearer to 0.

  • The cost function is convex, meaning there is a local minimum.

Gradient Descent for Logistic Regression

Surprisingly, the formulat for gradient descent in logistic regression looks the same to linear regression!

here

Useful References/Websites:

  1. https://numpy.org/doc/stable/
  2. https://numpy.org/doc/stable/user/basics.broadcasting.html

[ ] Todo: should learn python

[ ] Todo: clean the code above

TBC.

Mathematics for Machine Learning

A survey of Machine Learning

Attention is all you need

entry.title: 吹灭读书灯，一身都是月
entry.content:
吹灭读书灯，一身都是月，多美的句子啊。

小的时候最喜欢的房子就是：

小小的房间，与世人的喧嚣隔绝。屋子里的东西不多，一张朴素的床，一个放得满满的书架，一个能在晚上眺望星空的窗户，一张书桌。 比如曾经看过一本漫画书（忘记叫什么名字了），讲一个老爷爷和他的小精灵生活在一个农场里。那个小精灵的房子在田地的中央，每天晚上他就爬上去，打开昏黄的台灯，就着星光读书。

又比如里约大冒险里那个小男孩，住在深巷中的一个房子的天台上，甚至不能叫屋子，只是雨棚下的简单铺成的床。每天晚上，他听着巷子里的人声喧闹，爬着水管回到家中，从破烂的箱子里拿出一杯饮料，坐下来看星星看月亮。

而大学一学期的时光里，我最享受的时刻就是上完了课去完了cca回到宿舍里，洗完澡之后坐下来，煮一碗泡面，看着窗外的星光，伏案学习。这样的时光里，我感受不到时间的流逝。人生的享受不过如此。

在上大学之前我从来没住过宿，也从来没有远离过家乡，有时候一个人呆着难免会觉得孤独。当我试图通过互联网转移注意力、试图在无意义的社交中浪费时间，孤独感并不会得到缓解。唯有在这种夜深人静的时刻，吹灭读书灯一身都是月的时候，我才能清清楚楚明明白白地感受到生命的存在，感受到意义，消解孤独。

突然又想到一句话，好像和以上没什么联系：人的一生，苦也罢，乐也罢，的也罢，失也罢，要紧的是心中要有一泓清泉不能没有月辉。
