entry.title: Notes for CS2100
entry.content:
Notes for CS2100 - Computer Organization

C Programming

Introduction to C

Features of C

  1. Computer organisation is the study of internal working structuring and implementation of a computer system. It refers to the level of abstraction above the digital logic level, but below the operating system level.

  2. Why is C so fast?

    Edit code(vim tmp.c) -> Source code(tmp.c) -> Compile(gcc tmp.out) -> Executable Code(tmp.out) -> Output

  3. von Neumann architecture(Prinston Architecture)

    • Central Processing Unit

      • registers
      • A control unit containing an instruction register and program counter
      • Arithmetic/Logic Unit(ALU)

    • Memory Random Access Memory

    • Input Output Devices (I/O Devices)

  4. Why every varaible must be declared?

    The execution code for different variables in Source Code is different

  5. Data Types

    • int (\(2^{31}\) To \(-2^{31}\)) 4 bytes
    • float(4 bytes) or double(8 byte)
    • chars(8 bit integer)
    • Do note that an int doesn't always take up 4 bytes, it depends on the machine architecture

    sizeof( <variable> or <DataType> ) : returns the size of the variable

Sample C Programme

Computation

Selection Structures

Pointers and Functions

Pointers

Why do we use pointers!

  • When we pass the address of a variable into a function! See the Functions part of the blog.

Funcitions

TO use math functions, (1) include <math.h> (2) compile with -lm option

  1. It is generally a good practice to put a function prototypes at the top of the program before the main() function.

  2. Parameters are passed by value.

    • local variables inside a function only exist during the execution of a function. They are called automatic variables.

    • So if we want to access a value outside a function, we need to use pointers!

Array String and Structures

Array

  1. Handle homogeneous data(the same type). When declaraing an array, include the type, name and size.

  2. Array elements occupy contiuous memory locations.

String

  1. A string is an array of char with a null character '\0'(ascii value 0) at the end.

Structures

Structures allow grouping of heterogeneous members.

Number System

Introduction

Decimal Number System 1. Weighted-posistional number system 2. Base(radix) is 10 3. Each position has a weight of power of 10. (1.2 where 2 has a weight of \(2^{-1}\)) 4. e.g.: 7594.36 is a 6 digit number with 2 decimal places

Prefix 1. 0 for octal(8) 2. 0x for hexadecimal

Converting Decimals

American Standard Code for Information Interchange

  • 7 bits, plus 1 parity bit(odd or even parity) (Count the number of 1 bits and check whether it is even/odd(_0101101: odd parity should be 0))

  • Converting: '9' - '0' = 9

Signed and Unsigned Values

Ways to represent

  1. Sign-and-Magnitude

    • sign bit: 0 for + and 1 for -
    • \({10100000}_{sm} = 33_{10}\)

  2. 1s complment

    • -x = \(2^{n}\) - x - 1
    • to negate x, just invert all the bits
    • MSB still represents the sign, 0 for positive, 1 for negative
    • MSB's weight: (in n bits, ) \(-2^{n-1} + 1\)

  3. 2s-complement

    • -x = \(2^n\) - x
    • Negate x: invert all bits and add 1
    • shortcut: from right to left, copy everything until the first 1. invert everything to the left of the first 1. (e.g: 00101000 -> 11011000)
    • MSB still represent the sign, 0 for positive
    • in a 4-bit 2s number, the MSB has a weight of -8 ([U] Prove that?)

  4. Excess Notation

    • Allows the range of values to be split evenly betweent the positive and negative values.
    • the Excess-n notation means that in order for a negative number x to be negated, you need to add n to it. (e.g.: for the excess-4 notation, add 4 to -2 inorder to get 2)

For a 4-bit system, - SM: 0 to 7, -0 to -7 - 1s: 0 to 7, -0 to -7 - 2s: 0 to 7, -1 to -8 -8 is 1000

So

Complement on Fractions For fractions, simply ignore the '.'

Addition and Subtraction

2s Complement

Simply disgard the additional bit U

For subtraction, add \((-x)_{2s}\) U

Overflow Checking

If the carry in and carry out of the MSB is different, then a overflow occurs.

e.g.: 1001 + 1101 - in:0 out: 1 thus an overflow

1s Complement

  1. Addition: If there is a carry out of the MSB, add 1 to the result ([U] why does this work?)
  2. Subtraction: add \((-x)_{1s}\)

Representation of Numbers

Fixed vs Floating

  1. Fixed Point Representation The allocated number for the number part and fractional part are fixed.

  2. Floating Point Representation Allows representation of very large and very small numbers.

IEEE 754 Floating-Point Rep.

  1. Components:sign - exponent - mantissa(fraction)

The sign is for the mantissa, not the exponent

  1. Format: Single-precision(32 bits)

    • 1-bit sign, 8-bit exponent with bias127(excess-127), 23-bit mantissa

    • The Mantissa is normalised with an implicit leading bit 1.

      This means that we will always keep the part to the left of the point 1. Only the left part will be stored. e.g.: (110.1\()_2\) = 1.10\(1_2\) * \(2^2\), only 101 is stored in the mantissa. Normalisation: 123.2 \(10^{0}\) = 1.232 * \(10^{2}\)*

  2. E.g.: To find the IEEE 754 representation of -6.5 \[-6.5_{10} = -110.1_{2} = -1.101_{2} * 2^{2}\] Since \[Exponent = 2 + 127 = 129 = 10000001_2\] \[ (-110.1)_2 = 1_{sign}10000001_{exponent}{101 0000 ... 0000}_{mantissa}\] Or in hexadecimal \[1 10000001 1010000...00000_2 = C0D00000_{16}\]

ISA

MIPS((Microprocessor without Interlocked Pipelined Stages)

Introduction

  1. How the computer work:

    • programming: High-level language(C++/java) - Compiler -> Assembly Language - Assembler -> Machine Language Instructions(1000 1100...) -> MIPS processor

    • components: Processor & Memory.

      • Bus: the data connector between the Processor and Memory.

      • Processor: Fast. Contains ALU; Registers(temporary value storage)

        • Registers:

          • A typical architecture has 16 to 32 registers.

          • Registers have no data types(assumes that the data type is correct).

          • Referered by number($0)/name($a0)

          • $at(register 1) is reserved for the assembler, $k0-k1(26-27) reserved for the operating system

      • Memory: Instruction and data are stored in memory.

  2. ISA: abstraction between the hardware and low-level software(set of instructions) - manual for the hardware.

  3. What's the use?

    • Include everything programmers need to know to make the machine code work correctly.

    • allow CPU designers to design functionalities in different hardware

  4. Assembly Code Types:

    • Memory Instruction: move data into registers. r0 <- load i ; move data from register into memory: store

    • Reg-to-Reg Arithmetic: Arithmetic operations can work directly on registers only. It can also take in constant values(instead of from registers).

    • Control Flow: Instructions to change the control flow based on conditions(e.g. Repetition(loop) / Seection(if-else) ).

Instructions

Basic

  1. Arithmetic Operations

Arithmetic Operations are within registers only.

  1. Logical Operations

    • sll: shift left(<<) logical (4 bits: 1011 1000-> 1000 0000 (*))

    • srl: = divide by \(2^n\) (>>)

    • and: force bits to be 0; or: force bits to be 1s; nor: nor 0 to toggle bits. nor 1 to force bits to 0.; xor: xor 1 to get not operation

  2. Load instructions:

xor: true if a and b are different; nor: not or

More Instructions

Memory Organisation

  1. Given a k-bit address, the address space is of size \(2^k\), which is 8 locations. The memory might contain 1 byte of data in every location/address(byte addressing).

  2. Transfer Unit:

    • Using distinct memory address, we can access a single byte(byte addressable) / word (word accessable). * Word is Usually \(2^n\) bytes and is the common unit of transfer between processor and memory.

    • Word Alignment: Words are aligned in memory if they begin at a byte address that is a multiple number of bytes in a word. (e.g. If 4-byte word, then 0-3, 4-7 aligned; 1-4, 5-9 not aligned)

  3. MIPS is a load-store register architecture

    • 32 registers, each 32-bit long

    • Memory address are 32-bit long(\(2^{32}\) addresses)

    • Each word contains 32 bits (4 bytes) (\(2^{30}\) memory words)

Memory Instruction

  1. Load Word: lw $t0, 4($s0), the memory word at Mem[$s0 + 4] is loaded into $t0 4 is the offset and should be multiple of 4(length of memory)

  2. Store Word: sw $t0, 12($s0), value at $t0 is stored into Mem[$s0 + 12]

  3. Load and Store Arrays: $s3 contains the base address(A[0]), h is mapped to $s2

  1. lb and sb used to load byte and store byte (when $t2 is 32 bits long, we use the lowest byte.)

  2. ulw and uls are pseudo-Instructions for unaligned load word

Consecutive word address in machines with byte-addressing do not differ by 1, it differs by length of word!

Control Flows

Instruction Formats

MIPS Instruction Encoding

Each MIPS Instruction has a fixed length of 32 bits(word alligned).

Remember that the opcode and funct values on the sheet are hexadecimal!

MIPS Reference Data

  1. R format(Register): op $r1, $r2, $r3 (e.g.: srl sll add sub)

    • opcode rs rt rd shamt funct

    • only 32 registers, so only 5 bits for each registers. Same for shamt(), since shifting 32 bits clea the entire register. opcode and funct are 6 bits.

    • opcode are 0 for all R-Format.

    • funct specifies the instruction

    • rs: source register; rt: target register; rd: Destination register;

    • shamt: amount a shift instruction will shift by. 0 for all non-shift instructions

  2. I format(Immediate): op $r1, $r2, Immd (e.g.: lw sw beq bne)

    • opcode rs rt immediate

    • opcode (6 bits): uniquely specifies an instruction

    • rs rt (5 bits): rs: source register. rt: destination Note the difference in I and R format!

    • immediate(16 bits) : signed integer(2s complement).

    • For beq/bne, immediate is not enough to specify the entire target address(memory address: 32 bits). Thus, we do PC-Relative Addressing (a branch often change the PC value by a small amount)! When branch is not taken, PC = PC + 4, when taken, PC = (PC + 4) + immdiate * 4(1 word = 4 address). Where immediate is a signed two's complement integer (the number of instruction needed to jump).

    • Program Counter: a special register that keeps address of instruction to be executed in the processor.

  3. J format(Jump): op Immd

    • opcode target-address

    • opcode (6 bits)

    • target-addres (26 bits): Assume we go word by word, then it's 28 bits. Where do we get the other 4 bits? We choose the 4 most significant bits from PC + 4. Thus the maximum jump range would be \(2^28\) bytes = 256 MB. Effective Address: target-address + 00(by word)

    • Steps: write out address(in binary) of PC + 4, write out address to jump to. Take the middle 26 bits and store into target address(despite the 4 significant bits and the last 2 0s)

ISA Concepts

Overview

CISC vsRISC

  1. Complex Instruction Set Computer

    • Single Instruction performs comple operation in order to get smaller program size. No room for hardware optimization

  2. Reduced Instruction Set Computer

    • Keep instruction set small and simple. Burden on software to combine simpler operations to implement high-level statements.

Concept 1: Data Storage

  1. Storage Architecture:

    • Stack Architecture (Implicit)

    • Accumulator Architecture (Implicit)

    • General-purpose Register Architecture (Explicit) (most common choice) (CISC use mixture)

      • Register-memory architecture(one operand in memory)

      • Register-register(load-store) architecture (MIPS) (RISC use)

    • Memory-memory architecture: All operands in memory

Concept 2: Memory Address:

Concept 3 & 4: Operations in Instruction Set and Instruction Formats

Instruction Length

- Variable Lenght vs Fixed Length: Variable length instructions  are fetched by parts. The second part of the instruction is loaded due to the instruction of the 1st part...

- Very Long Instruction Word architecture: Instructions are 64 bit long and can load 32/64 bit instructions.

Concept 5: Instruction Encoding

Data Path

Fetch

  1. Fetch the instruction from the PC and output to the decoding stage.

  2. Elements:

    • Instruction Memory: a sequential circuit

    • Adder

    • Clock: [U] how do you implement this? what about the time needed for PC to be updated？ What is the point of having this?

Decode

  1. Elements:

    • Register File: collection of registers. Input the address and output data.

    • RegWrite: control signal to indicate whether the data in "Write Data" will be written into the Write Register

    • RegDst: Multiplexer that decides whether the write register reads from rt(0, I-Format instruction) or from Inst15:11.

    • ALUSrc: Multiplexer that decides what is written into Operand 2 of ALU(0 for read data2 and 1 for immediate value, note that for branch instruction, ALUSrc is set to 0 because Immediate value is fetched to the PC + 4 part). If we take immediate value, we extend it to 32 bits by filling in the 16 bits by the sign bit.(e.g. [U])

Execution Stage

  1. Functionalities

    • Arithmetic: add / sll / and

    • Memory: Address calculation

    • Branch: register comparison and target address calculation.

  2. Elements:

    • ALU(Arithmetic Logic Unit)

      • Control: 4-bit todecide the particular operation

      • isZero: output 1 if A op B == 0

    • Mutiplexer: Choose between PC + 4 or PC + 4 + offset.

      • PCSrc: 0 if it is the next Instruction, 1 if it is the next branch(PC + 4 + Immediate Value)

Memory Stage

  1. I/O: Fetch data fom ALU stage and output the results to be stored

  2. Elements:

    • Data Memory: why is this needed?

    • MemRead and MemWrite: only one is asserted at any time. If both are 1, error occur.

    • Write Data: for sw instructions, The src regiter's address is passed to WR and the content is fetched by RD2, then it's passed to the Write Data part and stored in the new address.

    • MemToReg: Decides whether the data should be read from memory (1) or from the ALU output(0).

Register Write Stage

  1. I/O: Input: computation result from memory/ALU

  2. Elements:

    • Register File

      • Write Register: takes in the address of which register to write in from the Decode Stage

      • Write Data: take in the data to write in

Elements

  1. Multiplexer: Control = k, select \(in_k\) as output.

Overview of Execution of a C programme

Compiler

Compile the C programme into MIPS assembly code.

  1. For if/else statement, in order to have shorter code, we inverse the code. E.g.:

Combinatory Circuit

Concept

We need a control unit to generate the signals for each multiplexer/Unite based on op-code and possibly function code. A combinatory circuit is a machine that changes the output immediately as the input changes. A Sequential Circuit is a machine that changes output only depending on a clock signal and the input(e.g. PC).

ALU

Introduction to ALU

ALU is a combinatory circuit.

  • If Ainvert = 1, the multiplexer for A would choose A'.

  • Operation: Choose from the 3 results: and (00) / or(01) / add(10)

Do note that for subtraction, in order to get A - B from A + B', we set the Cin to be 1. (in 2s complement)

  • We use A' to represent not A, use A.B to represent A and B, use A+B to represent A or B

Producing ALU signal

  1. Use opcode to generate ALUop signals

    • lw/sw: 00

    • beq: 01

    • R-type: 10

  2. Use ALUop and Function field to generate 4-bit ALUcontrol signal.

Clock Implementation

Multi-Cycle Implementation

  1. Single Cycle Implementation: for all kinds of instructions(sw/lw/beq), we select the slowest to have be the clock. In this case it's slow.

  2. Multi-Cycle Implementation: We break down the steps in the instruction(e.g. InstMem, Read from register, ALU operation...), choose the longest one as a cycle. This allows different instructions to be implemented in different times.([U] Why would this be faster? If this works, why don't we just divide the time into smaller steps?)

Pipelining

[U] TBC.

Assembly to Binary

  1. opcode 0: R format; 2: J format. Anything else: I format.

Boolean Algebra

Digital Circuits

Circuits that has two states: 1 and 0.

  • Combinational circuits: No memory, output depends solely on the input

  • Sequential: With memory, output depends on input and current state

Operators and Operations

Operators

Precedence from highest to lowest

  • . Not

  • . And

  • + Or

Do note the order is different to that in CS1231S

Duality: In an equation, if you swap all the + and ., swap all the 0s and 1s, the equation is still true.(E.g. \(x + 1 = 0 <-> x . 0 = 1\) or DeMorgan's Law)

Boolean Functions

Basics

Complement Functions: given a boolean function f, the complement function of that function f' is obtained by interchanging 0 and 1 in its output

Literals: boolean variables on its own or complemented form

SOP and POS

Product Term: the and of several literals or a single literal. E.g. x.y'.z

Sum Term: The or of several literals

Sum Of Product: the sum of several product terms or a single product term.

Product Of Sum: the prodcut of several sum terms or a single sum term.

Every boolean expression can be written as SOP / POS term.

Min and Max

Min Term: a min term of n variables is a product term of n literals.

  • The min and max term of 2 variables is named m0-m3:

    • m0: x'.y' / x+y

    • m1: x'.y / x+y'

    • m2: x.y' / x' + y

    • m3: x.y / x' + y'

mx' = Mx

A good way to memorize: imagine x' as 0 and x as 1. Then x'y' is 00, which is 00, x'y is 01, which is 1

Max Term: a max term of n variables is a sum term of n literals.

Canonical and Normal

Sum of min terms = Canonical SOP, we write this by looking at places where the output is 1.

Product of max terms = Canonical POS, we write this by looking at places where the output is 0.([U] check this out yourself!)

Converting Canonical SOP and POS: \[m0 + m1 + m5 = \sum{m(0,1,5)} = \prod{M(2,3,4,6,7)}\]

Logic Gates

  1. Logic Diagram

    • NAND: negating and. \[X nand Y = (X.Y)'\]

    • exclusive or(XOR): output 1 if different. \[X xor Y = X'.Y + X.Y'\]

    • XNOR: (XOR)' \[X xnor Y = X'.Y' + X.Y\]

    • NOR: \[X nor Y = (X+Y)'\]

  1. We call the (AND OR NOT) gate a complete set of logic(sufficient to build any gate). (NAND) or (NOR) gate is also a sufficient set of logic

    • We can easily implement SOPs using 2-level (AND OR) gates or 2-level (NAND) gates(NOT is not considered a level here). (AND OR) gate to (NAND) gate: simply change everything to NAND gates.

    • We can easily implement POSs using 2-level (AND OR) gates and 2-level(NOR) gates. ([U] Why!)

  2. Gray Code

    • A gray code sequence is a binary representation of a serires of codes, where the next code changes only 1 bit at a time. There are many gray code sequences. Generating a gray code sequence: Recursive! generate a 2-bit gray code sequence, copy the last one, add one to the front and flip the whole sequence!

  3. Delays:

    Given a logic gate with delay t, where inputs are stable at t1,t2,t3...etc.then the earliest time that the circuit would responese is \[max\{t_1,t_2, t_3,...\} + t\]

K-Maps

  1. ensure that minterms of adjascent cells differ by one literal. IOW, the rows and columns should be arranged in gray code.

    • Grouping: We can decrease literals in the final expression by grouping cells in the k map! However, we can only group \(2^n\) adjacent cells. Adjacent cells here refers to the wrapping around theory. We call th bigger groupings of minterms prime implicats. Essential Prim Implicants are PIs that are has at least one element tht is not covered by any other PIs.

  2. Finding the simplist SOP expression using K-Map!

    • mark all PIs

    • choose the EPIs

    • choose the rest of the PIs.

  3. Finding the simplist POS expression!

    • Draw the reversed K-map F'(flip the 0s and 1s)

    • Find the SOP of F', lets say it is F' = B.D' + A.B

    • F = (F')' = (B.D' + A.B)' = (B' + D).(A' + B')

  4. Don't Care Conditions: Conditions that we don't care. We use \(\sum{d}\) To show the set of don't care midterms. E.g. \[F(A,B,C) = \sum{m(3,5,6)} + \sum{d(0,7)}\] You can treat X as either 0 or 1.

Logic Circuits

  1. Decoder. Decode a n-bit code to m entities, where \(m <= 2^n\)

    • Decoders are enabled whenever E = 1 if they are one-enable or vice versa. We can use this feature to construct larger decoders using smaller decoders. E.g. To build a 3x8 decoder from 2 2x4 decoders and an inverter:

    • Normal Outputs: active high outputs; Negated Outputs: active low outputs(the output is the complement of the normal output)

  2. Encoder

    • Priority Encoder:

  3. Multiplexer

    • A \(2^n:1\) multiplexer is made from a \(n:2^n\) decoder and AND Gates.

Sequential Circuits

Basic Concepts

  1. Types:

    • Synchronous, where outputs changes at a specific time;

    • Asynchronous, where outputs changes at any time. (PRE(PRESET) is high, Q is set to high, CLR(CLEAR) = HIGH, Q is set to LOW)

  2. Notations:

    • We use \(A^+\) to denote the next stage of \(A\)

    • State Diagram: Each circle represents a present state, each arrow represents a transition, where the "0/1" represents input of 0 and output of 1(The output as the whole sequential circuit. There could be no outputs.).

    • We use 2 lettes to denote each flip-flop input: 1st letter denote the input name(J/K/S/R etc), second letter denote the name of the flip-flop

    • Remember the clock input

Synchronous Inputs

  • Pulse Triggered: Latches

    • Active High input S-R Latch: S for Set, R for Reset. If S and R are 0, no change will be made to Q. If S and R are 1, Q and Q' would be both low, which is invalid. Outputs change only when EN is high

    Do note that sometimes Q and Q' changes more than once so we need them to change until it is stable.

    • D-Latch: Make input R equal to S - there is no invalid state.

  • Edge Triggered: Flip-flops

    • Positive Edge-Triggered: A ">" symbol , where the circuit is triggered on the rising edge.

    • J-K Flip-Flop: When both J and K are High: the output is toggled.(Q and Q' are fed back to the NAND gate.)

    • T Flip Flop: J-K Flip Flop that makes J = K.

Design a Circuit

Memory

[U]

Pipelining

Basiccs

  1. Pipeline rate is limited by slowest pipeline stage and dependencies

    • The five execution stages in MIPS each takes 1 clock cycle.
- There will be additional registers between each stage. 

    - IF/IF: Register numbers for reading two registers; 16-bit offset to be sign-extendeed to 32-bit; PC + 4

    - ID/EX: Data Values from Register File; 32-bit immediate value; PC+4

    - EX/MEM: (PC+4+Immediate * 4); ALU result; isZero?; Data Read 2 from register

    - MEM/WB: ALU result; Mem Read data

- However, because of the pipeline design, the Write Register Number might be covered by the followed instructions. Thus, we need the Number to be carried through the five stages.

- Control Signals are generated in the decode stage. Thus, the control signals will be carried through the EX/MEM/WB stages(However, the signals for previous stages will not be carried once used.That is, signals for EX stage will not be carried in the EX/MEM register file.)

Performance:

  • Single-Cycle Datapath: Each instruction takes one cycle \[CT_{single} = max_{max(\sum{T_k})}\]

\[Time_{swq} = I * CT_{seq}\]

  • Multi-Cycle Processor

\[\text{Cycle Time: }CT_{multi} = max(T_k\text{(time for each stage)})\]

\[Time_{multi} = I * AverageCPI\text{(average clock per instruction)} * CT_{multi}\]

  • Pipelining Processor:

\[\text{Cycle Time: }CT_{pipeline} = max(T_k) + T_d\text{(time for accessing pipeline register)}\]

\[\text{Cycles needed for I instructions: }I + N -1 \text{N-1 cycles needed to fill up the pipeline}\]

\[\text{Time: } Time_{pipeline} = (I + N - 1 ) * CTpipeline\]

For ideal cases, where each stage takesthe sae amount of time, and there is no pipeline overhead(Td = 0), I >> N. \[Speedup_{pipeline} = \frac{Time_{No Pipeline}}{Time_{With Pipeline}} = N\]

  • Notes: \[1ns = 1 * 10^{-9} s \]

Hazards

  1. Structural Hazards

    • A hardware is used by two differet instructions!

      • Memory: split into instruction memory and data memory

      • Register: Split the stage into two halfs, where we write the register in the first half and read in the second half(We cannot swap this becuase the instructions are always based on previous instructions)

  2. Data Dependency

    • Read-After-Write Dependency: The only depndency that causes a pipeline hazard. Lets say data is written into register $2 instruct1, and it is read in instruction 2.

      • Solution: Forwarding: In instruction 2 execution stage, we override the data that was supposed to be read(that is, the original data before instruct1 was executed), and override it with the new data that is generated in the execution stage of instruct 1. This means that the data is passed to instruct 2 even before the write data stage of intruct1. The data is passed to the next instruction immmediately after execution.

      • Solution: Stall! What if data is needed before executed!

      • With only stall, we can only execute IF instruction as before, and ID instruction would be executed after the data is generated. With stall and forwarding, we can execute IF and ID as before, and we override data from ID technique in EX stage. That is, only EX stage will be delayed

  3. Control Dependency

    • Early Branch: We decide whether to branch in the instruction decode stage. This reduce the 3 clock waiting time to 1. However, this brings problem of data dependency since the data is needed in the ID stage, thus further stall is still needed.

      • If nothing, then 1 cc needed. If one add instruct before, then 2cc, if lw, then 3cc needed

    • Branch Prediction

      • Assume that branch is not taken. If prediction is wrong, we flush all the wronginstructions.

      • We combine this technique with the early branch technique to speed up(3cc delay -> 1cc delay).

      • This technique saves one delay everytime a correct prediction is made, in comparison to Early Branching

    • Delayed Branch:

      • Observation: Branch outcomes takes X number of Cycles to be know would cause X delay.

      • Move non-control dependent instructions into the X slots following a branch. This is done by the compiler. If no such instruction is find, then a no-op(NOP) instruction is put into the delay slot.

Things to say

Why do we even compare C with Python in this course?

entry.title: Thought - 2024.2
entry.content:
Thougts in Feb 2024

2.1 : opportunities will come!

2.2 : Tomorrow's a brand new day!

2.3 : Everything will be all right.

2.4 : Focus, even if it's one tiny thing.

2.5 : 戒骄戒躁。

2.6 : Keep Going

2.10 : 哎呀好像断更了。祝大家新年快乐！

2.12 : Try new things!

2.13 : Deal with loneliness

2.14 : 拓宽人脉！

2.18 : Making progress!

2.21 : guest lecture is fun.

2.22 : 自我承诺

2.23 : Try to make some BIG changes to life but seems to back fire. This is strange right? But when we try to change or step out of the comfort zone, things WILL NOT GO AS I WISH. I mean, what's the point of doing something, if this something is completely within my comfort zone? The real way to fulfill self promis is to treat it as a long term progress where I make mistakes. It's about not giving up and saying "let this be" when I fail at the first time. It's about continuing even if I want to just quit. It's sort of like when you cannot fulfill one promise that you made to your best friend. The real way to solve this is to say "here's how I can mend / I will do better next time, plz be patient with me" rather than feeling that this is the end of the commitment and friendship and say "Hey you know what, let's just put it there. Let's just abandon this friendship just because of this ONE mistake".

In short, treat yourself as a naughty child. Be patient with them and give them the right directions. Don't give up. Always move on.

5.19 Feel surrounded by love!

entry.title: Notes for CS2040S
entry.content:
Mainly questions that I encountered during my study for CS2040S

Lecture 1 & 2 : Java and Overall Data Structures

  1. [U] How to implement a File System?

  1. Objects represent state (“nouns”), not actions (”verbs”)

  2. What after folders?

  3. Objects should be unitary, without dividing functionality.(What does this mean and why?)

  4. First Principal of JAVA: Everything is an object

    A class is a template for producing an object.

    An object is an instance of the class.

  5. Second Principal of JAVA: Everything has a type

    Type String:

    • charAt(int index)

    • substring(int begin, int end)

    • toCharArray()

    • length()

    • compareTo(String otherString)

    • compareToIgnoreCase(String otherString)

    • equals(Object anObject)

  1. Behaviour is public, Data is private.

    regular variables/functions are PER OBJECT

    static variables/functions are PER CLASS // cannot access member variable

  2. Java program’s main method has to be declared static because the keyword static allows main to be called without creating an object. If omit the static keyword, it can still compile.

  3. Why is it incorrect to call Java "pass-by-reference" (should be called pass-reference-by-value instead)? In the following programme, i and j behaves differently. For j, lineA simply changes the address of the parameter j0 is pointing to. Hence, the value in j in main function does not change.(Think about the stack and heap structure!)

  4. f(n) = O(n) and g(n) = O(log n), what is h(n) = \(f(n)^{g(n)}\) ? the constat matters in a exponent! Cannot tell because g(n) = k log(n) matters in this situation.

    • Abstract Data Types (ADTs) are interfaces, which are specifications for behaviors without any implementation. E.g. Stack, Queue, Dictionary, Set, Map
    • Data Structures are concrete implementations of ADTs. E.g. Array, Linked List, Binary Tree, Hash Table, Heap
    • An ADT can be implemented by more than one data structure (E.g. Queue can be implemented using either an array or a linked list)

  7. Linked List

    • Advantages (compared to an array)
      • Variable size
      • Can easily add/delete elements anywhere
    • Disadvantages
      • Difficulty in random access
      • Not guaranteed to be stored together in memory

Lecture 3 & 4 : Peak Finding & Searching

  1. [U] 2D Peak Finding: How to PROVE that it works?

  2. Applications of binary search: Think of binary search whenever:

    • There is a sequence of "monotonic function" in blackbox. That is , for each v[i], for i <= k, it returns false, for i >= k, it returns true.
    • We try to find the k which is the seperating point of a "valid" value. (e.g. the "finding the pieces of homework-per-hour" problem 1 )

Lecture 5 & 6 : Sorting

BogoSort

  1. BogoSort Running Time

What is the expected running time of BogoSort?

Ans: O(n * n!) // The n is important!

  1. Quantumn BogoSort

Remember QuantumBogoSort when you learn about non-deterministic Turing Machines. What?

  1. Maybe BogoSort

MaybeBogoSort(A[1..n])

[U]What is the expected running time of MaybeBogoSort?

I guess it's something like 
    $\sum_{k = 1}^{n} (k-1) * (k-1)!$

[U]But then, What is the bigO notation of this thing?

Quicksort Optimization

  1. Switch to InsertionSort for small arrays.

  2. Halt recursion early, leaving small arrays unsorted. Then perform InsertionSort on entire array. (InsertionSort is fast on almost sorted arrays, about O(n))

  3. Java QuickSort Implementation uses a two pivot quicksort.

    • Sort the k pivots in the pivot array K
    • Do QuickSelect(e, K) on every element e in the original array (partitioning)
    • Recurse.

    The time complexity is T(n) = O(nlogk) + k * T(n / k) which is O(n log n). The performance of 2 and 3 pivot QuickSort are actually better than regular QuickSort because 2-pivot quicksort takes benefits of modern computer architecture.

Counting Sort

BitSort(?)

  1. Regular version
  • In-place-sorting agorithm for arrays of 0s and 1s: Do Quiksort Partition on the array, with two pointers, one at each side:The 0-pointer will advance to the right up until it finds the first 1; vice versa.
  • For an integer of k bits, do k times the In-place-sorting, starting from the highest bit.
  • time complexity is O(kn). However! since integers are stored in 64 bits, only when n > \(2^{64}\), this would beat Quicksort!
  1. Optimization:

  • Don't represent integers in binary, rather, in M - base numbering systems, where M is realtively small. Now, do Counting Sort \(log_M\) n times. the time complexity would be O(n * logM n). However, it still requires n to be relatively large.

  • TradeOff: Space! Lets say we use 8-base algorithm. It will take 256 Integers worth of space to do Counting Sort! Also the fact that the sorting is not in-place might take longer than you think ([U] Test it out!)

Keeping Trees Balanced

Concept

AVL Trees

Proof of Balance

  1. node v is height-balanced if |v.left.height – v.right.height| <= 1

  2. A height-balanced tree with n nodes has at most height h < 2log(n)

    Proof: = proof that every height-balanced tree with height h has at least n nodes (Since every height-balanced tree with height h has at least n > 2h/2 nodes, every height-balanced tree with n nodes has height h < 2log(n).)

     $$n_h >= 1 + n_{h-1} + n_{h-2} >= 2 * n_{h-2} >= 2^{n/2} * n_{n-n} = 2^{n/2}$$

Interesting Property: a maximal imbalanced AVL tree(AVL treewith the minimum possible number of nodes given its height h.)has all its subtrees to be maximally imbalanced.

B-trees

(a, b) trees

Rules

  • 2 ≤ a ≤ (b + 1)/2, where a and b refers to the minimum and maximum number of children in a internal node(not root / leaf).

  • every node should follow that: numberOfChild = numberOfKey + 1. For a node with k keys (v1,v2,...,vk), the subtrees(t1,t2,...,tk+1) should follow that ： First child t1 has key range ≤ v1； Final child tk+1 has key range > vk； All other children ti where i ∈ [2, k] has key range (vi−1, vi)

  • All leaves should have the same depth.

Manipulation

  1. Is ab tree balanced?

  2. Insertion:

Solution 1: (1) At first put the element in its correct place in the node (which is in sorted order)

  1. If the node overflows, then find the median ( = if is even and if is odd ) key in that node.

  2. Put the median key in the parent and split the current node

Solution 2: Proactive Approach

Preemptively split a node whe it is full so that it does not overflow in the future.

  1. Deletion:
  1. Case 1: Problem with Orphan Children.
In this case, we replace the deleted node either with the predecessor or successor key
  1. Case 2: Problem with downflow

If at least one sibling has at least a keys, then donate the key to the other sibling

If none have enough keys, then merge the two siblings and the parent together.

B-trees

B-trees are simply (B, 2B)-trees. That is to say, they are a subcategory of (a, b)-trees such that a = B and b = 2B.

  1. Cost

  • The cost of searching a B-tree / inserting / deleting in a B tree is O(loga n)!

Lecture 12 Hashing

Basic Concepts

  1. Difference with trees: no comparison between keys. [U] Why do you need O(n^2) time to sort in symbol table

  2. For java.util.Map<Key, Value>

    • No duplicates are allowed

  1. How does java implement hashFunctions?
  • hashCode should be:

  1. reflexive, symmetric, transitive

  2. consistent(always return the same answer)

  3. simultanious with equals method! (returns the same hashcode if and only if two objects are the same!)

  1. Two Good properties of a good Hash Function:

    • For every bucket j, there is some i such that h(key, i) = j. (make sure that you make use of all the space)

    • Simple Uniform Hashing Assumption: Every key is equally likely to be mapped to every permutation, independent of every other key. [U] wut? Why does linear probing violate the rule

Coping with Collision

Concept

Collision: h(k1) = h(k2), then k1 and k2 collide, where h is a hash function that takes in a key(e.g. "Hello") and returns the index where it should be stored in the array.

Chaining

  1. Why don't we just insert each key into a random bucket?

    • search would be slow!

    • Simple Uniform Hasing Assumption: every key is equally likely to map to every bucket.

      • What is the maximum chain length with SUHA? - O(log(n)) ([U] Why?)

      • To minimize the maximum chain length, we use two function h1 and h2. store the k in the bucket with a smaller chain. This would reduce the maximum chain length to O(log log n)

Open Addressing

  1. Concept: find the next bucket! one itme per slot and try to fill in all buckets.

  2. Idea: probe a sequence of buckets until you find an empty one.

    • h(key, i) where i is the time that you are trying (to find the slot), Do note that it may not be that h(key, i) = h(key, i - 1) + 1 and the "sequence" of objects may be arranged in random order.

  3. Problem: Deletion! If I simply set the box to null, when I try to search after deletion, I would stop at the deleted box and not search for any element after that(we assume that search stops as the first null, since we only stores elements in order). To solve that, we use DELETED for deleted elements instead of null.

  4. Linear probing:

    • Clusters! There are clusters and there is a higher probability for the next h(k) to hit the cluster

    • In practice, however, linear probing is faster. This is because caching!

  5. Double Hashing!

    • Use two hash functions f, g. \[h(k,i) = f(k) + i * g(k)\] ([U] Claim: if g(k) is relatively prime to m, then h(k,i) hits all buckets. L15)

  6. Performance(assuming uniform hashing) ([U] why do you need this? Proof? L15):

if \[\alpha = n/m\], then \[Cost = \frac {1}{1 - \alpha}\]

So performancee degrades badly as \(\alpha\) -> 1

Resizing

Concept

  1. I don't know how many elements are going to be inserted! - Grow the table!

  2. Idea: Implement a new slot m2 and a new hash function. Scan the items in m1 (old hash table) and insert them in a new hash table - O(n)

How fast to grow the hash table?

  1. m = m + 1: O(n^2); m = 2m: O(n); m = m^2: O(n^2)

  2. Shrinking and growing: if(n === m), then m = 2m. if(n < m / 4), then m = m /2

    • Claim: Everytime you double a table of size m, at least m / 2 new items were added.

    • Everytime you shrink a table of size m, at least m / 4 items were deleted.

  3. Technique of analyzing "average cost" (when some operations are cheap and some are expensive): Operation has amortized cost T(n) if for every integer k, the cost of k operations is <= kT(n)

    • e.g. For a list of insertions, the 5 insertions cost 5,5,5,13,7 respectively. The amortized cost = 7, where 5 < 7; 5 + 5 < 7 * 2; 5 + 5 + 5 < 7 * 3...etc

    • We claim that in this sense, the insert operation of a hash function has amortized cost O(1) [U] Proof

Priority Queue

Heaps

Two properties

  1. Heap Ordering: priority[parent] >= priority[child] (different from a binary search tree!)

  2. Complete Binary Tree:

    • Every level is full, except the last

    • All nodes are as far left as possible

Features

  1. The maimum hbeight of a heap with n elements: floor(log n)

  2. Storing: we store the tree in an array in BFS order. In this case, it is easier to access the last node and the root node.

  • How do we find the parent of a node? Since the tree is a Complete Binary Tree, the parent index would be: (k-1) / 2 !

Operations

  1. Insert:

    • Step one: add the new leaf to the left most of the last level

    • bubble up: swap the leaf that doesn't satify the Heap Ordering property with the parent. [U] Why would this work

  2. Increase Key: increase a key from e.g. 5 -> 25.

    • bubble up 25.

  3. Decrease Key : 28 -> 4

  • We bubble down with the child with the bigger one (22)

  1. Delete:

    • swap the node with the last node

    • delete the node

    • bubble down the last node

  2. extractMax: output the max and delete it.

Performancee:

All these operations are O(log n)!

Graph

Basic

Terminology

  1. Nodes: At least one; Edges: Each Edge connect two nodes(In hypergraph, each edge connects >=2 nodes in the graph)

  2. \[Graph G = <V,E>\], where V is a set of nodes, E is set of edges

  3. Simple path: set of edges connecting two nodes, which intersects eaach node at most once

    • Connected: all nodes is connected by a path

    • Cycle: where the last and first node are the same(not actually a path)

  4. Degree: number of adjacent edges.

  5. Diameter: Maximum distance between two nodes following the

  6. Cut of agraph is a partition of the vertices into two disjoint subssets.

    • Edge across the cut is the edge that has one node in each of the subsets

shortest path

  1. Directed Path: Each edge is directed

    • In-degree and Out-degree

  2. Weight: the cost to travel from v1 to v2 is w[v1][v2]. It can be a negative number

Different Kinds of Graphs

  1. Unrooted Tree: Connected graph with no cycles. Forest if not connected

  2. Star: one central node that has connection to all other nodes ([Do the foot of the stars need to be connected?])

  3. Clique(Complete Graph): All pairs are connected by edges(dimaeter: 1)

  4. Cycle: a circle.

  5. Bipartite: There exists a partition, where the edges only go from one edge to another. (Method to judge whether one graph is a bipartite: choose a random node and color it red, color the neighbours blue...etc)

  6. Planar graph: You can draw the graph in a way such that no edges intercross each other

Storing Data

  1. Adjacency Matrix : Graph represented as A[v][w] = 1 iff there is an edge between v and w. Neat property: A^2 = if there is length 2 paths([U] How does this work)

  2. Memory usage for graph G = (V, E)

    • Adjacency List: O(V+E)

    • Adjacency Matrix: O(\(V^2\))

    • if a graph is dense ( |E| = O(\(V^2\)) ) use adjacency Matrix

BFS and DFS

Concept

  1. Level: the 1st level consists of nodes that have path of length 1. The 2nd level consists of nodes that have path of length 2 but do not have path of length 1.

BFS

  1. Steps:

    • Build levels.(calculate level[i] from level[i - 1], skip already visited nodes)
  1. Complexity: O(V + E). Each vertex is added once and each edge is added once. (Do note the difference between E-edges and number of neighbours in each vertex here.)

DFS

Shortest Paths

  1. Key idea: triangle inequality \[cost[s][c] <= cost[s][a] + cost[a][c]\]

Shortest Path from s to every node

Bellman-Ford

Code
Proof

Suppose there is no negative-weight cylce ([S] What about those that don't satify? - Then it makes no sense calculating the shortest path, just keep going in circles![^4]) [^4]: In fact, you can use Bellman-Ford to try finding a negative weight cycle - just try to see whether edge is relaxed after n-1 iterations!

  1. Invariant: After j iterations(the outer loop), for any j-hop node u, cost[u] stores the correct answer. j-hop node refers to the node u where on the shortest path from s to u, there are j edges.

  2. Proof by Induction

    • Base case: After 1st iteration, all 1-hop nodes stored the correct value \[cost[u] = weight(s,u)\]

    • If \[cost[hop_{j - 1}] = ultimate\_value\] after one iteration, the edge connecting (j-1)hop nodes and j-hop nodes are relaxed. Thus \[cost[hop_j] = ultimate\_value\]

    • Since all nodes in Graph are at most n-1 hop nodes (since there is no cylce in the ultimate shortest path)

  3. Performance: O(VE). If weight of all edges are the same, then just use BFS*

Dijkstra

Basic Idea

  1. Steps: Maintain a priority queue, which stores the keys and is prioritized with the current estimated dist[k]. Maintain an array vis[], where vis[k] = true if the node k is visited, or dist[k] stores the correct answer.

    • Initialize: change all nodes to be unvisited and all \(dist[k] = infinity\). Put the starting node into the priority queue and change dist[s] = 0.

    • Loop: For each loop, extract the minimum element in priority queue, lets say m. mark m as visited(we'll explain why dist[m] stores the correct answer in a second)

    • Relax all edges connected to m. Put the nodes which are not visited in the queue, update the nodes that are in the queue

    • Repeat the Loop step.

[U] How did they come up with such a genius idea! Rethink about the thinking process.

Proof

Imagine all the nodes on the graph to be of three categories: visited nodes(nodes that already have the currect distance stored), fringe nodes (unvisited nodes that have at least one edge that are connected to visited nodes), and unknown nodes (these are nodes whose information are completely unknown and don't have any edge connected to the visited nodes). As shown in the picture below.

Before the proof, there are four properties that a graph would hold.

  1. Any subpath of a shortest path is a shortest path. (Proof by contradiction)

  2. All fringe nodes are in the priority queue.

  3. All shortest paths that ends with an unvisited node would involve at least one visited node and one fringe node.

  4. At anytime, for any node v, \[curr\_dist[v] >= dist[v]\]

Now, we proof by induction: If at anytime, suppose for the visited elements, all distance stored are correct, then the next element that we delete in the priority queue would store the correct dist[m].

We will prove this by contradiction:

  • Suppose we delete the minimum element from the priority queue, m, the dist[m] stores the wrong value. However, the "shortest path" that involves m is the orange path shown in the picture.

  • By property(3), suppose the first fringe node that the shortest path involves is w. Since m is removed from priority queue(with property(2)), \[curr\_dist[m] > curr\_dist[w]\tag{1}\]

  • However, since m is in the shortest path to w([U] Is this true? does curr_dist[m] really stores the correct value?), \[curr\_dist[m] = dist[m]\tag{2}\], by property(1), \[dist[m] > curr\_dist[w]\tag{2.5}\]. Since we know that m is in the shortest path to w, we say \[dist[m] <= dist[w]\tag{3}\], we conclude that \[dist[w] >= curr\_dist[w] \tag{4}\], which is contradictory to property(4).

The proof only works for weightsthat are all positive. If there is negative weight edges, formula(3) will not hold. This is different from Bellman-Ford Algorithm

Performance Analysis

Time Complexity: we insert / deletMin for |V| times each, since every node is added to the priority queue oncec. We decreaseKey at most |E| times. Since priority queue operations are O(log V) \[runtime = O((V+E)logV) = O(E log V)\]

  • Priority Queue by AVL tree(with Hash Table): O((V + E) log V) = O(E log V)

    • insert: O(log n)

    • deleteMin(): O(log n)

    • decreaseKey(key, priority): O(log n)

    • contains(key): O(1)

  • Performance iseven vetter if use d-way Heap or Fibonacci Heap(?)

  1. For Trees, we can relax in the order of BFS/DFS!

Weight Union

  1. Functionalities

    • Union(i, j)

    • isConnected(i, j): return whether i and j are in the same union

    • Union-Split-Find: for weight unions that implements this functionality, check THIS

  2. Idea: store data in a tree structure. Each tree is a union. When union i, j, find the root of i and j and merge the two trees.

    • Find(i) : find the root of i

    • Merge(rt1, rt2): union the two trees. We merge trees by setting the parent of the root of the smaller tree to be the root of the other tree.

  3. Performance

    • isConnected(i, j) and Union(i, j) : O(h) / O(log n)

    • Proof that h = O(log n) : Proof that for any tree of height h has at least \(2^k\) nodes. Proof this by induction: suppose for height k trees this is true, proof that for height k+1 trees this is true.

    • improvement: set parent of every node that is traversed to be the root node (flattens the tree - path compression!)

    • Starting from empty, any sequence of m union/find operations on n objects takes: O(n + mα(m, n)time., where α is the inverse arckermann function ([U] ? Proof and some knowledge about the function? Tarjan 1975)

  4. Application

    • Binomial tree

Minimum Spanning Trees

Basics

  1. Spanning Trees: In a weighted, undirected graphs([U] Why is it more complicated with directed graphs), spanning tree is an acyclic subset of the edges that connects all nodes.

  2. Minimum Spanning tree: a subset of a undirected weighted graph which connects all nodes and has the least total weight.

Properties

Assumption: For simplification, assume all edge weights are distinct.

  1. No cycles are in the MST.

  2. Cut Property: if you cut an edge on a minimum spanning tree, then the rest two components T1 and T2, would still be the MST for the respective nodes.(However, T1 MST + T2 MST + Minimum edge != (T1 + T2)MST)

  3. Cycle Property: For any cycle on the original graph, the maximum weigt edge is not in the MST.

    • Proof: Proof by contradiction - If the maximum weight edge is in the MST. By property 1, at least 1 edge of the cycle is not in the MST, lets all these edges Enot. By property 2, if we cut the maximum weight edge, the rest of the two parts are still MSTs, then if we add one edge that connects the two parts, it would have a smaller weight. Now we just need to proof that there is at least one Enot that connects the two parts. ([U] how do you prove this? By cycle property?)

  4. For every partition of the nodes, the minimum weight edge across the cut is in the MST. (However, it might not be the only one!)

    • For every vertex, the minium outgoing edge is always part of the MST.

Generic MST Algorithm

  1. Apply the red rule or vlue rule to an arbitrary edge until no more edges can be colored.

    • Red Rule: If C isa cycle with no red arcs, then color the max-weight edge in C red. (Property 2)

    • Blue Rule: If D is a cut with no blue arcs, then color the min-weight edge in D blue. (Property 4)

  2. Proof of the greedy algorithm:

    • On termination, every cycle has a red edge, and the blue edges form a tree(if not, then there is a cut with no blue edge). Every edge is colored. ([U]Why, can't there be an eedge that neither satisfy the red rule, nor satisfy the blue rule? )

MST Algorithms

Prim

  1. Basic Idea:

    • S: set of nodes connected by blue edges.

    • Repeat: Identify the cut: {S, V - S}

    • Find minimum weight edge on cut (Priority queue)

    • Add new node to S

[U] In the code for prim, why should the priority queue keep track of the nodes rather than simply keep track of the weights?

Kruskal

  1. Basic idea:

    • sort edges by weight from smallest to biggest.

    • Consider edges in ascending order:

      • If both endpoints are in the same blue tree, then color the edge red. (Must be the heaviest edge in the loop!)

      • else, color the edge blue.

Variations of MST

  1. What if all edges have the same weight?

    • DFS / BFS!

  2. What if all edges have weight of {1...10} ?

    • In kruskal algorithm, we no longer need to sort the edges, we just use array of size 10. (Why does thi make time complexity \(O(\alpha{E})\))

    • In Prim's algorithm, we use array of size 10 as the priority queue. Where slot A[j] stores a linked list of nodes of weight j. The time complexity would be \(O(E)\)([U] Go through thi).

[U] Why would this fail for Dijkstra's Algorithm?

  1. Directed Minimum Spanning Trees

    • Special Case: For a directed acyclic graph with one "root", We can generate MST by adding minimum weight incoming edge for every node except the root([U] Why?)

  2. Maximum Spanning Trees:

    • Only relative weight matters.

    • multiply each edge by -1 (why does this work)

Dynamic Programming

Basic

  1. Optimal substructures

  2. Overlapping sub-problems: smaller problems are used to solve multiple different bigger problems.

  3. Check the Floyd Warshall Problem

Graph Summary

[U] Where should I put Topological sort, under shortest paths or parrallel? Should it be named Topological sort or DAG?

Problems (in tutorials)

Mountain stack

A mountains consists of n hills, aligned linearly. Each hill i, stands at a height of a[i]. Standing at hill i, you’ll see a point j kilometers away if and only if all hills from i−j to i are no taller than a[i] meters. For each hill i, determine the maximum distance you can see from it(distance is "-1" if no hill blocks your sight). (Hint: try using a stack to achieve O(n) time complexity)

Answer: Use a monotonic decreasing stack:

  • For every element at index i in the array:

[U] This is somehow incorrect. But the idea is similar. pls correct this.

How to come up with this: Gather only the information you need! Information for any hill i that is lower than another hill j where j > i can be eliminated!

Chicken Rice Competetion :

  • You have in front of you n plates of chicken rice, each plate contains n bites of chicken rice. Supppose you want to find the median chicken rice. Design an algorithm to maximize the amount of remaining chicken rice on the median plate.
  • Solution 1: Run QuickSelect: on one level of QuickSelect, the expectation of time of bitten on any plate is (1 / n) * (n - 1) + (1 - 1 / n) * 1 <= 2. Thus the expected consumption of chicken rice is O(2 log n) bites.
  • Solution 2: Inserting each plate into an AVL tree takes O(log n) comparisons. (However, if median is the root of AVL tree, there would be O(n) moves!)
  • In fact, using a randomized algorithm, this can be solved with only O(log log(n)) bites to the median plate, but that is much, much more complicated [U]

Weight Selection(Eonomic Research):

  • You are an economist doing a research on the wealth of different generations. Each row of data you've gained consists of a unique identifier, an age, and a number that represents their amount of wealth. Your goal is to divide the dataset into “equi-wealth” age ranges. That is, given a parameter k, you should produce k different age ranges A1, A2, ..., Ak with the following properties:
    1. All the ages of people in set \(A_j\) should be <= the ages of people in \(A_{j+1}\).
    2. The sum of wealth in each set should be (roughly) the same (tolerating rounding errors if k does not divide the total wealth, or exact equality is not attainable).

You should assume that the given k is relatively small while the dataset is very large . (i.e. O(nk) < O(n log n))

Design the most efficient algorithm you can to solve this problem. Optionally, consider the case where rows can consist of the same ages, versus the case where rows have unique ages. - Solution: a weight selection problem. We are asking to find the 1/k,2/k,...,k−1/k order statistics of the weighted sum 1. Finding a single Weight Order Statistic. - First, use QuickSelect to find the median aged dataset, and then partition around the median - sum the totals on the left and right halves. (DO the sum while partitioning to save time!) If target weight > left sum, subtract left sum weight from your target weight and search on right half...etc. This is O(n) time. 2. repeat for the k - 1 targets and Do one final partitioning to build your output lists([U] why is this nessary) This is O(nk) time.

  • Solution 2: Instead of finding the median, we use a random pivot. Each time we partition, we keep track of the sum of the wealth to the left of the selected pivots! We do Quickselect O(k) times and each selection takes O(n) times. ([U] Needs further explanation, see tutorial slides w6 "better solution") [U] Why is this better than Solution 1?

  • Solution 3: Find k order statistics all at once! When random pivot was selected, say its 2 * \(\bar{w}\), then we recurse on the left to find the 1st order-statistics and recurse on the right to find the 3rd order-statistics! In this case, we get a rough overall runtime of O(n log k) ([U] Needs further explanation, see tutorial slides w6 "better solution")

  • [U] How about naiive solution?

Height of Binary Tree After Subtree Removal

  • Description: You are given a binary tree with n nodes. You are given m queries, where queries[i] asks the height of the tree after deletion of the subtree containing the ith node. What is the optimized solution?

  • Solution 1 : Build a tree. for each query, walk up the tree and try to update the node's height. O(log n * m) ([U] Why does the slides say its O(n * m)?)

  • Solution 2: Depth of a node refers to the number of edges in the simple path from root to the node

    1. preprocess the tree to get its depth using DFS and height using in-order-traversal.
    2. For ith query, we look at all the nodes with the same depth of ith node and find the maximum height among them. The answer to the query would be node[i].depth + max_height.
    3. SO how do we group the nodes of the same depth? We do this by iterating the nodes in in-order-traversal([U] wut?). For each group of nodes in the same depth, we only require the maximum height and second maximum height. To do this we need O(n) time([U] wut?).
    4. In this algorithm, we only need O(1) for each query. The overal time complexity would be O(n + m)

[U] There is another method based on Euler Tour Tree (which is out of syllabus of CS2040S). You may find more details about the data structure on

https://courses.csail.mit.edu/6.851/spring07/scribe/lec05.pdf and http://web.stanford.edu/class/archive/cs/cs166/cs166.1166/lectures/17/Small17.pdf.

The data structure provide a neat alternative solution and support for deleting multiple subtrees.

Safely Evaluating average pay!

  1. Question 1
  • Come up with an algorithm that will allow the students to determine the average pay of the group without revealing their own salaries to any of the other members in the group
  • Solution 1: Everyone pick a random number and add it to there salery. 1st person passes the sum to the 2nd person, the 2nd person add their sum to the number the 1st one passed ... Until everyone's sum adds up. Then they deduct the number one by one.
  1. Extention:
  • what happens if k members of the group decide to collude and share information. Is your algorithm still able to preserve the privacy of all the members, or could the salary for some of the members be leaked
  • Solution 1 will not work! the (i-1)th and (i+1)th person can easily calculate the ith people's salery! ([U] is there a way to manipulate the order of adding and subtracting such that there is no way they can do that?)
  • Solution 2: Divide the salery into n pieces and each person give the ith piece of their salery to the ith people! Even if everyone in the house except for the kth person cheat and try to calculate kth person's salery, they cannot do so! ([U] How did you come up with that!)
  • Way to come up with a good algorithm: [U] ?
    1. How you (re)define the problem
    2. Determines what are the metrics of success
    3. How to iterate a solution?

Pancake flipping!

  1. Description: Try to serve pancakes a stack sorted by size. You can only flip the pancakes at the top of the pancake stack.

  2. Show that any pancake flipping algorithm requires at least n flips. What do we call such properties of the problem? [U] Check out recitation 2

  • A lower bound of the algorithm!
  • Consider a possible worst case! Why is this the worst case? [U] Worst case should differ for different algorithms?
    • Feature 1: if two consecutive pancakes in the sorted pancake stack are together in the unsorted stack then we can treat those pancakes as one pancake either with burnt side up or down based on their order. This means that if 2 4 are adjacent in the original pancake, we must use at least one flip between 2 and 4 to seperate them!
    • Feature 2: If the largest pancake is at the bottom we can simply ignore it.
    • By Feature 1, we need at least n - 1 flips. By feature 2, we need at least 1 additional flip to seperate the bottom one and the table! Thus the lower bound is at least n flip.

[U] Check out recitation 2 on more about pancake flipping.

DNA Swapping

  1. Description
  • We are doing sorting when only calculating the cst of swapping subarrays. The cost of swapping subarrays of length k would cost O(k).
  1. What if we are sorting an array of two element? - Do mergesort!
  2. What if the input string comprises of arbitrary characters without duplicates.
  • Wait, we can do this using the solution in 2! partition is the same as sorting an array of two kinds of elements! The time complexity is T(n) = 2 * T(n / 2) + O(n log n). [U]

Random Permutation

  1. Given an array of n items, we need to return a random permutation of the given array.

  2. How do we evaluate the performance of a random permutation algorithm!

  • The possibility of each permuatation to appear in the array should be n!

  1. Consider the Fisher-Yates / Knuth Shuffle algorithm: Time Complexity O(n) and Space complexity O(1)! [U] why is space complexity O(1)

  2. Solution 2: assign a random number to each element and sort it!

  • Question: what if we meet duplicate numbers?
  • If the sorting is stable, simply treat the earlier occurence to be the smaller one([U] Is this still random? )
  • If not, the sorting would not be stable!

Binary Counter Amortized Cost

  1. Suppose we have a binary counter, after n increments, what is the amortized cost?

  2. Simple answer: O(log n). But this is not the case!

  3. Closer look: 2! [U] Seems kind of unclear

    • Observation: each increament would only change one 0 to 1.

    • Suppose each bit is a bank. When we change from 0 to 1, add 1 dollar, when we change from 1 to 0, pay 1 dollar.

[U] Check the ppt

Scheduling

  1. Suppose we have a list of tasks, some of the has constraints like A has to be done at least 10 minitues before B, C must be done after D, E must be done at most 20 minutes after F...etc

  2. Think of this as a DAG problem! Imagine the tasks as nodes and the edges as constraints of the nodes, dist[u] means the shortest time needed to complete u.

    • For constraint: A has to be done at least 10 minutes before B. this means \(dist[A] + 10 <= dist[B]\) since in graphs we only have \[dist[u] <= dist[v] + weight[u,v]\], we transform the constraint to be \[dist[A] <= dist[B] - 10\], therefore, the edge between A and B would be \(-10\).

    • Now, we add Starting point S with edges of weight 0 connected to all nodes and run Bellman-Ford on S.

    • Time Complexity: \(O(n * m)\), where n, m is the number of tasks and constraints respectfully. ([U] Why don't we use topological sort?)

Puzzles:

  1. [U]Sicherman Dice

  2. [U]Puzzle of the Week: Bubbly (Courtesy: MIT Puzzle Hunt, 2019)

  3. [U]Treasure Island:

You choose a set of keys.

You can test whether all the good keys are in the set (and possibly

some extra keys). If even one bad key is in the set, then it fails.

Goal: identify exactly the good keys.

  1. PotW: Gambling for Profit? (Lecture 07 p121)

  2. Prisoners! (L10)

** Updated Until:

Lecture 11 p103

tut 2

[U] optional questions in tutorials.

[U] Check the open resourse

[U] Check this out: visualAlgo

[U] HashTable - Tutorial Slides W6 - problem 2 -credit CS2109S

[U] Optional problems in tuts and recs

There are some useful advice on midterms in Week 6 tut materal

  1. You have n piles of homework and the ith pile has piles[i] pieces of homework. You have h hours left before all your homework is due. Try to find out the minimal k = “pieces of homework”-per-hour while finishing everything on time that follows the below constraints: At every hour, choose a pile of homework, If the pile has less than k pieces of homework remaining, you do not start on the next pile during the same hour. how you can find the minimum integer k such that you can finish all your homework withinh hours? (Hint: v[i] is the function of whether you can finish on time, and k is the seperating point. )↩︎

  2. Another way to prove this is : ↩︎

  3. \(\color{green}{SOMETHING RELATED}\): How many flips to get at least one head? : \[E[X] = \frac{1}{2}(1) + \frac{1}{2} (1 + E[X]) \tag{1}\] (this is useful for infinity E[x] problems)↩︎

entry.title: Cheat Sheet for Unix CLI
entry.content:
Learnt Unix in NUS CS2030S. Here's a cheatsheet.

Original Text Here

Background

  • Ctrl + D : end of input/ exit shell

  • Ctrl + Z : suspend the current running program. This pauses the execution of the program (but not terminates it). Resume executing in the background with the command bg Back to execution in the foreground again with the command fg.

  • Ctrl + C : terminate the current running program.

  • Ctrl + S : freeze the terminal. Ctrl + Q : resume the terminal.

  • Ctrl + P & Ctrl + N: See the previously executed commands.

  • Tab : complete the name of a command/file

  • man : look at the manuals. e.g. : man ls

Essentials

Directory

Symbols

  • ~ : The home directory. ~bob means the home directory of a user named bob.

  • . : Current working directory.

  • /: Root directory.

  • .. : Parent directory.

Path

  • Absolute Path: /home/o/ooiwt

  • Relative Path: ../o/ooiwt

  • pwd : Print current working directory.

  • ls : Check the list of contents. Contents that starts with . are hidden, use ls -a to check all contents. ls -F to see more information. ls -l to see information including the size and date.

  • mkdir : make a directory

  • cd : brings you to the home directory

Files

  • rmdir : remove a subdirectory, it must be empty.

  • cp :

    cp -r copys the whole directory: $ cp -r ../workshop .

* If there is an existing file with the same name, cp will overwrite the existing file without warning.

  • mv : Rename a content.

    When the destination of mv is a directory, then it moves the source to the destisnation

    It's better to use mv -i as it asks if you want to overwrite a file

  • rm : removes the file.

    *It's better to use rm -i.

  • cat : Take a look at the content of a file.

    less : look at less content of a file.

      In less, use `space` to move forward,  `b` to move back, `q` to quit.

File Permission

What:

  • r : read - 4

  • w : write - 2

  • x : executing a file / access contents and info of the directory - 1

Permission are used in two ways:

  1. rwx or r-x etc.

  2. Numerical notation like : 5 = r-x

Who

u is the user who owns the file; g refers to the users in the same group as the user; and o are all the other users.

For instance, the permission of 644, or rw-r--r--, on a file means that:

- the owner can read and write

- the group users can only read

- all the other users can only read

Check and Change of Permission

Check:

$ ls -l test.txt
-rw-r--r--@ 1 ooiwt  staff  64 Jul 28 09:52 test.txt

Change:

$ chmod 666 test.txt
$ ls -l test.txt
-rw-rw-rw-@ 1 ooiwt  staff  64 Jul 28 09:52 test.txt

Or:

$ chmod o-w test.txt
$ ls -l test.txt
-rw-rw-r--@ 1 ooiwt  staff  64 Jul 28 09:52 test.txt

Eliminates the writing permission of a user.(use o+w to give the permission)

Standard Input & Output

  • wc : it will count the number of lines, words, and characters in that given file. e.g.:
  • > & >> : The operators > and >> are used to redirect the standard output to a file. The difference is that > will overwrite the given file, while >> will concatenate into the given file. e.g.:

This programme reads the output of wc text.txt into a file test.count

  • < & << : Imports the input.

Advanced

To be continued.

entry.title: Notes for SICPJS
entry.content:
Notes for SICP JS.

Introduction

SICPJS is a Java Script version(or Source Language) of the computer science textbook Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs (SICP) . The original textbook was written by Massachusetts Institute of Technology professors Harold Abelson and Gerald Jay Sussman with Julie Sussman, and is known as the "Wizard Book" in hacker culture. In NUS, year1 CS students who take CS1101S uses SICPJS as the reference book on their amazing study journey with the Source Academy.

During my study of CS in 2023/24 Sem 1 in CS1101, I find SICPJS a great book. It not only works as an introductory book to computer science, but also inspired me to think about the fundamental principles of CS. I wrote this note during my sem1 study in CS1101S and is still working on the book after that. Hopefully, this note would have been finished in 2023/24 Sem2.

This note involves ideas that I find inspiring in the book and questions that I came up with while reading. Notes with (U) are questions that are unsolved and it would be great if readers could comment below about your ideas on the notes.

I recommend anyone studying CS to read the book(either the original version or JS version), and hopefully this note would be of some use to the reader's understanding of the book.

Notes

Foreword

  1. (U)What is combinatory calculus & lambda calculus & predicate calculus?

    Lambda Calculus : (lambda x.lambda y. x+y)(5 6) = 11

    A lambda notation is equivalent to a Turing Machine, vice versa

  2. (U)How can you use a lambda notation to represent AND and OR?

    For reference, if we define True and False as:

     True: lambda x. lambda y. x

 False: lambda x. lambda y. y

    For any boolean value b, the logic NOT can be represented as

     NOT: lambda b. b False True

    Proof: case 1: b = True

             Not b = Not True = True Flase True = False

     case2: b = False

         Not b = Not False = False False True = True

  3. (U)What is object oriented approach?

  4. (U)Why does the author say that most programming languages “does not have a completely automatic storage management”?

  5. (U)Is it better to have 100 functions programmed on 1 data structure or 10 functions for each of 10 data structures?

  6. (U)What is Lisp?

Chapter 1 Building Abstractions with Functions

1.1

Inflix Notation

operand + operator + operand

JS programmers know the value of everything but the cost of nothing.(LOL)

The fact that every expression has a value and JS programmers are known for not caring about time/space efficiency.

(U)What is the difference between statements and expressions?

A?

Snake Case & Camel Case

Different ways to name variables.

Chapter 3: Modularity, Objects, and State

3.1

Why use the CSE machine, why is environment an important thing?(3.1.2)

Storing the temporary data that the function needs in the local environment of a function helps us to generalize functions(for example, in this way the memoization progress can be applied to different functions)

Encapsulation: We say a variable is encapsulated in the environment when its information is only accessed inside the environment. A general systemword designing principle called “the hiding principle“ is that a system gets more modular and robust by providing information access to only the parts of the programme that are “need to know”. 

Monte Carlo Method

The use of randomness. Say you want to calculate the area of a circle(x^2 +y^2 <= 1). Choose a rectangle(whose area you have already know), and try to make many following experiments: pick up one point and test whether the point is in the circle. If we try this many times, we get the possibility of a point in the rectangle is in the circle, thus p * area_of_rectangle = area_of_circle.
